questions.questionHtml: A wire of length l carries a steady current. It is bent first to form a circular plane loop of one turn. The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is B. The same length is now bent more sharply to give a double loop of smaller radius. The magnetic field at the centre caused by the same is
questions.solutionText: Solution The wire of length l is bent to from a circular loop, so 2 π r = l ⇒ r = l 2 π The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is B = μ 0 I 2 r = μ 0 I × 2 π 2 l Now the same length of the wire is bent to from a double loop ∴ 2 × 2 π r ′ = l ⇒ r ′ = l 4 π And the magnetic field at the centre B ′ = μ 0 I × 2 2 × r ′ = 2 μ 0 I 2 × l 4 π = 2 × 4 π μ I 2 l ∴ B ′ B = 4 ⇒ B ′ = 4 B
questions.solution: Solution The wire of length l is bent to from a circular loop, so 2 π r = l ⇒ r = l 2 π The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is B = μ 0 I 2 r = μ 0 I × 2 π 2 l Now the same length of the wire is bent to from a double loop ∴ 2 × 2 π r ′ = l ⇒ r ′ = l 4 π And the magnetic field at the centre B ′ = μ 0 I × 2 2 × r ′ = 2 μ 0 I 2 × l 4 π = 2 × 4 π μ I 2 l ∴ B ′ B = 4 ⇒ B ′ = 4 B
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The wire of length l is bent to from a circular loop, so 2πr=l
⇒r=l2π
The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is
B=μ0I2r=μ0I×2π2l
Now the same length of the wire is bent to from a double loop
∴2×2πr′=l⇒r′=l4π
And the magnetic field at the centre
B′=μ0I×22×r′=2μ0I2×l4π=2×4πμI2l∴B′B=4⇒B′=4B

questions.questionHtml: The angular speed of a body changes from ω1 to ω2 without applying a torque, but due to changes in moment of inertia. The ratio of radii of gyration in the two cases is
questions.solutionText: Solution No torque ⇒ angular momentum conserved: I 1 ω 1 = I 2 ω 2 I = M k 2 where k is radius of gyration (mass M is constant) M k 1 2 ω 1 = M k 2 2 ω 2 k 1 2 k 2 2 = ω 2 ω 1 k 1 k 2 = ω 2 ω 1 k 1 : k 2 = ω 2 : ω 1 Answer: (3) ω 2 : ω 1
questions.solution: Solution No torque ⇒ angular momentum conserved: I 1 ω 1 = I 2 ω 2 I = M k 2 where k is radius of gyration (mass M is constant) M k 1 2 ω 1 = M k 2 2 ω 2 k 1 2 k 2 2 = ω 2 ω 1 k 1 k 2 = ω 2 ω 1 k 1 : k 2 = ω 2 : ω 1 Answer: (3) ω 2 : ω 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
No torque ⇒ angular momentum conserved: I1ω1=I2ω2

I=Mk2 where k is radius of gyration (mass M is constant)

Mk12ω1=Mk22ω2

k12k22=ω2ω1

k1k2=ω2ω1

k1:k2=ω2:ω1

Answer: (3) ω2:ω1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The graph between the maximum speed v max of a photoelectron and frequency ( v ) of the incident radiation, in photoelectric effect is correctly represented by:
questions.questionText: The graph between the maximum speed v max of a photoelectron and frequency ( v ) of the incident radiation, in photoelectric effect is correctly represented by:
questions.question: The graph between the maximum speed v max of a photoelectron and frequency ( v ) of the incident radiation, in photoelectric effect is correctly represented by:
questions.prompt: The graph between the maximum speed v max of a photoelectron and frequency ( v ) of the incident radiation, in photoelectric effect is correctly represented by:
questions.html: The graph between the maximum speed vmax of a photoelectron and frequency (v) of the incident radiation, in photoelectric effect is correctly represented by:
questions.questionHtml: The graph between the maximum speed vmax of a photoelectron and frequency (v) of the incident radiation, in photoelectric effect is correctly represented by:
questions.solutionText: Solution From Einstein's photoelectric equation: 1 2 m v m a x 2 = h ν − ϕ v m a x = 2 ( h ν − ϕ ) m This means v m a x ∝ ν − ν 0 , which is a square-root function of ν . Key features of the graph: v m a x = 0 for ν ≤ ν 0 (threshold frequency) ⇒ flat line along x-axis until ν 0 After ν 0 , v m a x increases as a square-root curve (concave downward, starts steep then flattens) Graph (3) shows exactly this: zero until threshold, then a concave-down square-root rise. Answer: (3)
questions.solution: Solution From Einstein's photoelectric equation: 1 2 m v m a x 2 = h ν − ϕ v m a x = 2 ( h ν − ϕ ) m This means v m a x ∝ ν − ν 0 , which is a square-root function of ν . Key features of the graph: v m a x = 0 for ν ≤ ν 0 (threshold frequency) ⇒ flat line along x-axis until ν 0 After ν 0 , v m a x increases as a square-root curve (concave downward, starts steep then flattens) Graph (3) shows exactly this: zero until threshold, then a concave-down square-root rise. Answer: (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
From Einstein's photoelectric equation:
12mvmax2=hν−ϕ

vmax=2(hν−ϕ)m

This means vmax∝ν−ν0, which is a square-root function of ν.

Key features of the graph:
vmax=0 for ν≤ν0 (threshold frequency) ⇒ flat line along x-axis until ν0
After ν0, vmax increases as a square-root curve (concave downward, starts steep then flattens)

Graph (3) shows exactly this: zero until threshold, then a concave-down square-root rise.

Answer: (3)

questions.solutionText: Solution Each magnet has magnetic moment M = m l . The two magnets are placed perpendicular to each other (at right angles), so their magnetic moment vectors are at 90 ° to each other. Net magnetic moment: M n e t = M 2 + M 2 = 2 M = 2 m l Answer: (3) 2 m l
questions.solution: Solution Each magnet has magnetic moment M = m l . The two magnets are placed perpendicular to each other (at right angles), so their magnetic moment vectors are at 90 ° to each other. Net magnetic moment: M n e t = M 2 + M 2 = 2 M = 2 m l Answer: (3) 2 m l
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Each magnet has magnetic moment M=ml.

The two magnets are placed perpendicular to each other (at right angles), so their magnetic moment vectors are at 90° to each other.

Net magnetic moment:
Mnet=M2+M2=2M=2ml

Answer: (3) 2ml

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Transitions between three energy levels in a particular atom give rise to three spectral lines of wavelengths, in increasing magnitudes λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3. Which one of the following equations correctly relates λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3 ?
questions.questionText: Transitions between three energy levels in a particular atom give rise to three spectral lines of wavelengths, in increasing magnitudes λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3. Which one of the following equations correctly relates λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3 ?
questions.question: Transitions between three energy levels in a particular atom give rise to three spectral lines of wavelengths, in increasing magnitudes λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3. Which one of the following equations correctly relates λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3 ?
questions.prompt: Transitions between three energy levels in a particular atom give rise to three spectral lines of wavelengths, in increasing magnitudes λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3. Which one of the following equations correctly relates λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3 ?
questions.html: Transitions between three energy levels in a particular atom give rise to three spectral lines of wavelengths, in increasing magnitudes λ1, λ2 and λ3. Which one of the following equations correctly relates λ1, λ2 and λ3?
questions.questionHtml: Transitions between three energy levels in a particular atom give rise to three spectral lines of wavelengths, in increasing magnitudes λ1, λ2 and λ3. Which one of the following equations correctly relates λ1, λ2 and λ3?
questions.solutionText: Solution Let the three energy levels be E 1 , E 2 , and E 3 . The wavelength λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3 of the spectra lines corresponding to the three energy transitions are depicted as shown in figure. E = hf = hc λ or E ∝ 1 λ (given λ 1 < λ 2 < λ 3 ) Thus, for the three wavelengths, we have E 3 - E 2 = hc λ 3 … i E 2 - E 1 = hc λ 1 … ii E 3 - E 1 = hc λ 1 … iv Now, E 3 - E 1 = E 3 - E 2 + E 2 - E 1 ⇒ hc λ 1 = hc λ 3 + hc λ 2 ⇒ 1 λ 1 = 1 λ 2 + 1 λ 3
questions.solution: Solution Let the three energy levels be E 1 , E 2 , and E 3 . The wavelength λ 1 , λ 2 and λ 3 of the spectra lines corresponding to the three energy transitions are depicted as shown in figure. E = hf = hc λ or E ∝ 1 λ (given λ 1 < λ 2 < λ 3 ) Thus, for the three wavelengths, we have E 3 - E 2 = hc λ 3 … i E 2 - E 1 = hc λ 1 … ii E 3 - E 1 = hc λ 1 … iv Now, E 3 - E 1 = E 3 - E 2 + E 2 - E 1 ⇒ hc λ 1 = hc λ 3 + hc λ 2 ⇒ 1 λ 1 = 1 λ 2 + 1 λ 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Let the three energy levels be E1,E2, and E3. The wavelength λ1,λ2 and λ3 of the spectra lines corresponding to the three energy transitions are depicted as shown in figure.

E=hf=hcλ or E∝1λ
(given λ1<λ2<λ3)
Thus, for the three wavelengths, we have
E3-E2=hcλ3…i
E2-E1=hcλ1…ii
E3-E1=hcλ1…iv
Now, E3-E1=E3-E2+E2-E1


⇒hcλ1=hcλ3+hcλ2⇒1λ1=1λ2+1λ3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The principle of controlled chain reaction is used in
questions.questionText: The principle of controlled chain reaction is used in
questions.question: The principle of controlled chain reaction is used in
questions.prompt: The principle of controlled chain reaction is used in
questions.html: The principle of controlled chain reaction is used in
questions.questionHtml: The principle of controlled chain reaction is used in
questions.solutionText: Solution A controlled chain reaction is one where the rate of fission is deliberately regulated to release energy at a steady, controlled rate. Atom bomb → uncontrolled chain reaction (rapid, explosive) Core of sun → nuclear fusion, not fission chain reaction Artificial radioactivity → induced radioactivity by bombardment, not a chain reaction Atomic energy reactor → uses controlled chain reaction (via control rods) to produce steady heat for electricity generation Answer: (1) Atomic energy reactor
questions.solution: Solution A controlled chain reaction is one where the rate of fission is deliberately regulated to release energy at a steady, controlled rate. Atom bomb → uncontrolled chain reaction (rapid, explosive) Core of sun → nuclear fusion, not fission chain reaction Artificial radioactivity → induced radioactivity by bombardment, not a chain reaction Atomic energy reactor → uses controlled chain reaction (via control rods) to produce steady heat for electricity generation Answer: (1) Atomic energy reactor
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
A controlled chain reaction is one where the rate of fission is deliberately regulated to release energy at a steady, controlled rate.

Atom bomb → uncontrolled chain reaction (rapid, explosive)
Core of sun → nuclear fusion, not fission chain reaction
Artificial radioactivity → induced radioactivity by bombardment, not a chain reaction
Atomic energy reactor → uses controlled chain reaction (via control rods) to produce steady heat for electricity generation

Answer: (1) Atomic energy reactor

questions.text: The maximum velocity of a simple harmonic motion represented by y = 3 sin ⁡ ( 100 t + π 6 ) m is given by
questions.questionText: The maximum velocity of a simple harmonic motion represented by y = 3 sin ⁡ ( 100 t + π 6 ) m is given by
questions.question: The maximum velocity of a simple harmonic motion represented by y = 3 sin ⁡ ( 100 t + π 6 ) m is given by
questions.prompt: The maximum velocity of a simple harmonic motion represented by y = 3 sin ⁡ ( 100 t + π 6 ) m is given by
questions.html: The maximum velocity of a simple harmonic motion represented by y=3 sin⁡(100t+π6)m is given by
questions.questionHtml: The maximum velocity of a simple harmonic motion represented by y=3 sin⁡(100t+π6)m is given by
questions.solutionText: Solution The given equation is written as, y = 3 sin ⁡ 100 t + π 6 ...(i) The general equation of simple harmonic motion is written as y = a sin ⁡ ( ω t + ∅ ) ...(ii) Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get a = 3 , ω = 100 Maximum velocity, v = a ω​ = 3 × 100 = 300 m s - 1 `
questions.solution: Solution The given equation is written as, y = 3 sin ⁡ 100 t + π 6 ...(i) The general equation of simple harmonic motion is written as y = a sin ⁡ ( ω t + ∅ ) ...(ii) Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get a = 3 , ω = 100 Maximum velocity, v = a ω​ = 3 × 100 = 300 m s - 1 `
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The given equation is written as,
y=3sin⁡100 t+π6 ...(i)
The general equation of simple harmonic motion is written as
y=asin⁡(ωt+∅) ...(ii)
Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
a=3, ω=100
Maximum velocity, v=aω​ =3×100=300 m s-1
`

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A 90-watt red light bulb and a 60-watt green light bulb are turned on. Which bulb emits photons with the greater energy?
questions.questionText: A 90-watt red light bulb and a 60-watt green light bulb are turned on. Which bulb emits photons with the greater energy?
questions.question: A 90-watt red light bulb and a 60-watt green light bulb are turned on. Which bulb emits photons with the greater energy?
questions.prompt: A 90-watt red light bulb and a 60-watt green light bulb are turned on. Which bulb emits photons with the greater energy?
questions.html: A 90-watt red light bulb and a 60-watt green light bulb are turned on. Which bulb emits photons with the greater energy?
questions.questionHtml: A 90-watt red light bulb and a 60-watt green light bulb are turned on. Which bulb emits photons with the greater energy?
questions.solutionText: Solution Energy of a photon: E = h ν = h c λ Green light has shorter wavelength (higher frequency) than red light. λ g r e e n < λ r e d ⇒ E g r e e n > E r e d The wattage (90W vs 60W) refers to the total power (number of photons per second × energy per photon), not the energy of individual photons. Individual photon energy depends only on frequency/wavelength, not on power of the bulb. Answer: (3) The green bulb, because a green photon has a greater energy than a red photon. `
questions.solution: Solution Energy of a photon: E = h ν = h c λ Green light has shorter wavelength (higher frequency) than red light. λ g r e e n < λ r e d ⇒ E g r e e n > E r e d The wattage (90W vs 60W) refers to the total power (number of photons per second × energy per photon), not the energy of individual photons. Individual photon energy depends only on frequency/wavelength, not on power of the bulb. Answer: (3) The green bulb, because a green photon has a greater energy than a red photon. `
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Energy of a photon: E=hν=hcλ

Green light has shorter wavelength (higher frequency) than red light.
λgreen<λred ⇒ Egreen>Ered

The wattage (90W vs 60W) refers to the total power (number of photons per second × energy per photon), not the energy of individual photons. Individual photon energy depends only on frequency/wavelength, not on power of the bulb.

Answer: (3) The green bulb, because a green photon has a greater energy than a red photon.
`

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The P-V diagram of a gas undergoing a cyclic process (ABCDA) is shown in the graph, where P is in units of N m -2 and V in cm 3 . Identify the incorrect statement.
questions.questionText: The P-V diagram of a gas undergoing a cyclic process (ABCDA) is shown in the graph, where P is in units of N m -2 and V in cm 3 . Identify the incorrect statement.
questions.question: The P-V diagram of a gas undergoing a cyclic process (ABCDA) is shown in the graph, where P is in units of N m -2 and V in cm 3 . Identify the incorrect statement.
questions.prompt: The P-V diagram of a gas undergoing a cyclic process (ABCDA) is shown in the graph, where P is in units of N m -2 and V in cm 3 . Identify the incorrect statement.
questions.html: The P-V diagram of a gas undergoing a cyclic process (ABCDA) is shown in the graph, where P is in units of N m-2 and V in cm3. Identify the incorrect statement.
questions.questionHtml: The P-V diagram of a gas undergoing a cyclic process (ABCDA) is shown in the graph, where P is in units of N m-2 and V in cm3. Identify the incorrect statement.
questions.solutionText: Solution From the graph: P A = P B = 2 × 10 5 Nm − 2 , P C = P D = 1 × 10 5 Nm − 2 V A = V D = 2.0 cm 3 = 2 × 10 − 6 m 3 , V B = V C = 4.0 cm 3 = 4 × 10 − 6 m 3 A → B (isobaric expansion): W = P Δ V = 2 × 10 5 × ( 4 − 2 ) × 10 − 6 = 0.4 J (done by gas). Statement (1) is correct. C → D (isobaric compression): W = P Δ V = 1 × 10 5 × ( 2 − 4 ) × 10 − 6 = − 0.2 J (done on gas = 0.2 J). Statement (2) is correct. B → C (isochoric, Δ V = 0 ): W = 0 . Statement (3) is correct. D → A (isochoric, Δ V = 0 ): W = 0 , so no work is done by or on gas from D to A. Statement (4) says work is done by gas from B to C and on gas from D to A — both parts are incorrect (no work in either isochoric process). Answer: (4) is the incorrect statement ^
questions.solution: Solution From the graph: P A = P B = 2 × 10 5 Nm − 2 , P C = P D = 1 × 10 5 Nm − 2 V A = V D = 2.0 cm 3 = 2 × 10 − 6 m 3 , V B = V C = 4.0 cm 3 = 4 × 10 − 6 m 3 A → B (isobaric expansion): W = P Δ V = 2 × 10 5 × ( 4 − 2 ) × 10 − 6 = 0.4 J (done by gas). Statement (1) is correct. C → D (isobaric compression): W = P Δ V = 1 × 10 5 × ( 2 − 4 ) × 10 − 6 = − 0.2 J (done on gas = 0.2 J). Statement (2) is correct. B → C (isochoric, Δ V = 0 ): W = 0 . Statement (3) is correct. D → A (isochoric, Δ V = 0 ): W = 0 , so no work is done by or on gas from D to A. Statement (4) says work is done by gas from B to C and on gas from D to A — both parts are incorrect (no work in either isochoric process). Answer: (4) is the incorrect statement ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
From the graph:
PA=PB=2×105 Nm−2, PC=PD=1×105 Nm−2
VA=VD=2.0 cm3 =2×10−6 m3, VB=VC=4.0 cm3 =4×10−6 m3

A → B (isobaric expansion):
W=PΔV=2×105×(4−2)×10−6=0.4 J (done by gas). Statement (1) is correct.

C → D (isobaric compression):
W=PΔV=1×105×(2−4)×10−6=−0.2 J (done on gas = 0.2 J). Statement (2) is correct.

B → C (isochoric, ΔV=0):
W=0. Statement (3) is correct.

D → A (isochoric, ΔV=0):
W=0, so no work is done by or on gas from D to A.
Statement (4) says work is done by gas from B to C and on gas from D to A — both parts are incorrect (no work in either isochoric process).

Answer: (4) is the incorrect statement
^

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
RT=R0(1+αT)

At T=100°C, R=100Ω:
100=R0(1+0.005×100)=R0(1.5)
R0=1001.5=2003Ω

At R=200Ω:
200=2003(1+0.005T)
3=1+0.005T
0.005T=2
T=400°C

Answer: (4) 400°C

questions.solutionText: Solution Mutual inductance between two coils is given as M = e 1 d i 2 d t , where e 1 is the emf induced in first coil due to rate of change of current d i 2 d t , in second coil which causes rate of change of magnetic flux in first coil. So, when rate of change of current in second coil is 1 A s - 1 , emf induced in first coil is called mutual inductance between the coils. ;

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Mutual inductance between two coils is given as

M=e1di2dt, where e1 is the emf induced in first coil due to rate of change of current di2dt, in second coil which causes rate of change of magnetic flux in first coil.

So, when rate of change of current in second coil is 1 A s-1, emf induced in first coil is called mutual inductance between the coils.

questions.text: Two spheres ' S 1 ' and ' S 2 ' have radii ' R ' and '3R', temperature ' T ' K and. T 3 K respectively. If 3 they are coated with a material of same emissivity, rate of radiation of ' S 1 ' is E then rate of radiation of ' S 2 ' is _______. (spheres are of the same material)

questions.questionHtml: Two spheres ' S1 ' and ' S2 ' have radii ' R ' and '3R', temperature ' T ' K and. T3 K respectively. If 3 they are coated with a material of same emissivity, rate of radiation of ' S1 ' is E then rate of radiation of ' S2 ' is _______. (spheres are of the same material)
questions.solutionText: Solution Rate of radiation (Stefan's law): P = ε σ T 4 × A = ε σ T 4 × 4 π R 2 For S 1 : radius = R , temperature = T E = ε σ T 4 × 4 π R 2 For S 2 : radius = 3 R , temperature = T 3 P 2 = ε σ ( T 3 ) 4 × 4 π ( 3 R ) 2 = ε σ ⋅ T 4 81 × 4 π ⋅ 9 R 2 = ε σ T 4 × 4 π R 2 × 9 81 = E × 1 9 = E 9 Answer: (3) E 9 *
questions.solution: Solution Rate of radiation (Stefan's law): P = ε σ T 4 × A = ε σ T 4 × 4 π R 2 For S 1 : radius = R , temperature = T E = ε σ T 4 × 4 π R 2 For S 2 : radius = 3 R , temperature = T 3 P 2 = ε σ ( T 3 ) 4 × 4 π ( 3 R ) 2 = ε σ ⋅ T 4 81 × 4 π ⋅ 9 R 2 = ε σ T 4 × 4 π R 2 × 9 81 = E × 1 9 = E 9 Answer: (3) E 9 *
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Rate of radiation (Stefan's law): P=εσT4×A=εσT4×4πR2

For S1: radius =R, temperature =T
E=εσT4×4πR2

For S2: radius =3R, temperature =T3
P2=εσ(T3)4×4π(3R)2

=εσ⋅T481×4π⋅9R2

=εσT4×4πR2×981

=E×19=E9

Answer: (3) E9
*

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A point object is placed in air on the principal axis of a convex spherical refracting surface of radius R = 1 0 c m . The surface separates air ( μ 1 = 1 ) from a glass medium ( μ 2 = 1.5 ). The object is placed at a distance of 3 0 c m from the pole of the surface. A thin lens is placed coaxially completely inside the glass medium at a distance of 40 cm from the pole of the spherical surface. Using the Cartesian sign convention, determine the nature and focal length (when immersed in the glass medium) of the thin lens required so that the final image is formed at infinity.
questions.questionText: A point object is placed in air on the principal axis of a convex spherical refracting surface of radius R = 1 0 c m . questions.html:
A point object is placed in air on the principal axis of a convex spherical refracting surface of radius R=10 cm. The surface separates air ( μ1=1 ) from a glass medium ( μ2=1.5 ). The object is placed at a distance of 30 cm from the pole of the surface.

A thin lens is placed coaxially completely inside the glass medium at a distance of 40 cm from the pole of the spherical surface. Using the Cartesian sign convention, determine the nature and focal length (when immersed in the glass medium) of the thin lens required so that the final image is formed at infinity.
questions.questionHtml:
A point object is placed in air on the principal axis of a convex spherical refracting surface of radius R=10 cm. The surface separates air ( μ1=1 ) from a glass medium ( μ2=1.5 ). The object is placed at a distance of 30 cm from the pole of the surface.

A thin lens is placed coaxially completely inside the glass medium at a distance of 40 cm from the pole of the spherical surface. Using the Cartesian sign convention, determine the nature and focal length (when immersed in the glass medium) of the thin lens required so that the final image is formed at infinity.
questions.solutionText: Solution Refraction at spherical surface (air → glass): μ 2 v − μ 1 u = μ 2 − μ 1 R u = − 30 cm, R = + 10 cm, μ 1 = 1 , μ 2 = 1.5 1.5 v − 1 − 30 = 1.5 − 1 10 1.5 v + 1 30 = 0.5 10 = 1 20 1.5 v = 1 20 − 1 30 = 3 − 2 60 = 1 60 v = 90 cm So the refracted rays converge at 90 cm from the pole (inside glass). This acts as a virtual object for the thin lens placed at 40 cm from the pole. Object distance for lens: u L = 90 − 40 = 50 cm ⇒ u L = + 50 cm (rays converging toward it, so virtual object) For final image at infinity: v L → ∞ Lens formula: 1 v L − 1 u L = 1 f 1 ∞ − 1 50 = 1 f 1 f = − 1 50 f = − 50 cm A negative focal length lens immersed in glass medium is a concave lens. Answer: B) Concave lens, f = − 50 cm !
questions.solution: Solution Refraction at spherical surface (air → glass): μ 2 v − μ 1 u = μ 2 − μ 1 R u = − 30 cm, R = + 10 cm, μ 1 = 1 , μ 2 = 1.5 1.5 v − 1 − 30 = 1.5 − 1 10 1.5 v + 1 30 = 0.5 10 = 1 20 1.5 v = 1 20 − 1 30 = 3 − 2 60 = 1 60 v = 90 cm So the refracted rays converge at 90 cm from the pole (inside glass). This acts as a virtual object for the thin lens placed at 40 cm from the pole. Object distance for lens: u L = 90 − 40 = 50 cm ⇒ u L = + 50 cm (rays converging toward it, so virtual object) For final image at infinity: v L → ∞ Lens formula: 1 v L − 1 u L = 1 f 1 ∞ − 1 50 = 1 f 1 f = − 1 50 f = − 50 cm A negative focal length lens immersed in glass medium is a concave lens. Answer: B) Concave lens, f = − 50 cm !
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Refraction at spherical surface (air → glass):

μ2v−μ1u=μ2−μ1R

u=−30 cm, R=+10 cm, μ1=1, μ2=1.5

1.5v−1−30=1.5−110

1.5v+130=0.510=120

1.5v=120−130=3−260=160

v=90 cm

So the refracted rays converge at 90 cm from the pole (inside glass). This acts as a virtual object for the thin lens placed at 40 cm from the pole.

Object distance for lens: uL=90−40=50 cm ⇒uL=+50 cm (rays converging toward it, so virtual object)

For final image at infinity: vL→∞

Lens formula: 1vL−1uL=1f

1∞−150=1f

1f=−150

f=−50 cm

A negative focal length lens immersed in glass medium is a concave lens.

Answer: B) Concave lens, f=−50 cm
!

Now an external force F is applied on the block so that the spring is stretched by an additional length of 2 m. The work done by the force F is [Take g=10 m s-2]
questions.solutionText: Solution Initial stretch: x 0 = m g k = 100 100 = 1 m, additional stretch x = 2 m W F = Δ P E s p r i n g − W g r a v i t y Δ P E s p r i n g = 1 2 k [ ( x 0 + x ) 2 − x 0 2 ] = 1 2 × 100 × ( 9 − 1 ) = 400 J W g r a v i t y = m g x = 100 × 2 = 200 J W F = 400 − 200 = 200 J Answer: (1) 200 J ^
questions.solution: Solution Initial stretch: x 0 = m g k = 100 100 = 1 m, additional stretch x = 2 m W F = Δ P E s p r i n g − W g r a v i t y Δ P E s p r i n g = 1 2 k [ ( x 0 + x ) 2 − x 0 2 ] = 1 2 × 100 × ( 9 − 1 ) = 400 J W g r a v i t y = m g x = 100 × 2 = 200 J W F = 400 − 200 = 200 J Answer: (1) 200 J ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Initial stretch: x0=mgk=100100=1 m, additional stretch x=2 m

WF=ΔPEspring−Wgravity

ΔPEspring=12k[(x0+x)2−x02]=12×100×(9−1)=400 J

Wgravity=mgx=100×2=200 J

WF=400−200=200 J

Answer: (1) 200 J
^

questions.questionHtml: Three capacitors each of capacity 4μF are to be connected in such a way that the effective capacitance is 6μF. This can be done by:
questions.solutionText: Solution (1) All in series: 1 C = 1 4 + 1 4 + 1 4 = 3 4 ⇒ C = 4 3 μ F. No. (2) All in parallel: C = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12 μ F. No. (3) Two in series, then parallel with third: Two in series: 1 C ′ = 1 4 + 1 4 ⇒ C ′ = 2 μ F Parallel with third: C = 2 + 4 = 6 μ F. Yes. (4) Two in parallel, then series with third: Two in parallel: C ′ = 4 + 4 = 8 μ F Series with third: 1 C = 1 8 + 1 4 = 3 8 ⇒ C = 8 3 μ F. No. Answer: (3)
questions.solution: Solution (1) All in series: 1 C = 1 4 + 1 4 + 1 4 = 3 4 ⇒ C = 4 3 μ F. No. (2) All in parallel: C = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12 μ F. No. (3) Two in series, then parallel with third: Two in series: 1 C ′ = 1 4 + 1 4 ⇒ C ′ = 2 μ F Parallel with third: C = 2 + 4 = 6 μ F. Yes. (4) Two in parallel, then series with third: Two in parallel: C ′ = 4 + 4 = 8 μ F Series with third: 1 C = 1 8 + 1 4 = 3 8 ⇒ C = 8 3 μ F. No. Answer: (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(1) All in series: 1C=14+14+14=34 ⇒ C=43μF. No.

(2) All in parallel: C=4+4+4=12μF. No.

(3) Two in series, then parallel with third:
Two in series: 1C′=14+14 ⇒ C′=2μF
Parallel with third: C=2+4=6μF. Yes.


(4) Two in parallel, then series with third:
Two in parallel: C′=4+4=8μF
Series with third: 1C=18+14=38 ⇒ C=83μF. No.

Answer: (3)

questions.solutionText: Solution As given in the question, a ring of mass m is clamped on the periphery of a light circular disc and a small particle having equal mass is clamped at the centre of the disc and centre of the system is rotating in a circle of radius r with uniform speed v . The centre of mass of the ring coincides with the centre of disc, therefore, the total mass of system rotating in the circle is 2 m . The external force must be acting on the system to provide the centripetal force required for rotation. F ext = F c ∴ F ext = 2 m v 2 r

Solution
As given in the question, a ring of mass m is clamped on the periphery of a light circular disc and a small particle having equal mass is clamped at the centre of the disc and centre of the system is rotating in a circle of radius r with uniform speed v.

The centre of mass of the ring coincides with the centre of disc, therefore, the total mass of system rotating in the circle is 2m.

Fext=Fc

questions.text: A steel wire of cross-sectional area A breaks under a stretching force F . If the wire is cut into two equal halves, what is the force required to break one of the halves?
questions.questionText: A steel wire of cross-sectional area A breaks under a stretching force F . If the wire is cut into two equal halves, what is the force required to break one of the halves?

questions.prompt: A steel wire of cross-sectional area A breaks under a stretching force F . If the wire is cut into two equal halves, what is the force required to break one of the halves?
questions.html: A steel wire of cross-sectional area A breaks under a stretching force F. If the wire is cut into two equal halves, what is the force required to break one of the halves?
questions.questionHtml: A steel wire of cross-sectional area A breaks under a stretching force F. If the wire is cut into two equal halves, what is the force required to break one of the halves?
questions.solutionText: Solution Breaking stress of the wire: σ = F A (material property, independent of length) When cut into two equal halves, cross-sectional area A remains the same. Force required to break the half = σ × A = F A × A = F Answer: B) F
questions.solution: Solution Breaking stress of the wire: σ = F A (material property, independent of length) When cut into two equal halves, cross-sectional area A remains the same. Force required to break the half = σ × A = F A × A = F Answer: B) F
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Breaking stress of the wire: σ=FA (material property, independent of length)

When cut into two equal halves, cross-sectional area A remains the same.

Force required to break the half = σ×A=FA×A=F

Answer: B) F

questions.text: If w 1 , w 2 and w 3 represent the work done in moving a particle from A to B along three different paths 1, 2 and 3 respectively(as shown)in the gravitational field of a point mass m. Find the correct relation between w 1 , w 2 and w 3
questions.questionText: If w 1 , w 2 and w 3 represent the work done in moving a particle from A to B along three different paths 1, 2 and 3 respectively(as shown)in the gravitational field of a point mass m. Find the correct relation between w 1 , w 2 and w 3
questions.question: If w 1 , w 2 and w 3 represent the work done in moving a particle from A to B along three different paths 1, 2 and 3 respectively(as shown)in the gravitational field of a point mass m. Find the correct relation between w 1 , w 2 and w 3
questions.prompt: If w 1 , w 2 and w 3 represent the work done in moving a particle from A to B along three different paths 1, 2 and 3 respectively(as shown)in the gravitational field of a point mass m. Find the correct relation between w 1 , w 2 and w 3
questions.html: If w1, w2 and w3 represent the work done in moving a particle from A to B along three different paths 1, 2 and 3 respectively(as shown)in the gravitational field of a point mass m. Find the correct relation between w1, w2 and w3
questions.questionHtml: If w1, w2 and w3 represent the work done in moving a particle from A to B along three different paths 1, 2 and 3 respectively(as shown)in the gravitational field of a point mass m. Find the correct relation between w1, w2 and w3
questions.solutionText: Solution Gravitational force is a conservative force. For conservative forces, work done is path-independent — it depends only on the initial and final positions (A and B), not on the path taken. Since all three paths connect the same points A and B: w 1 = w 2 = w 3 Answer: (2) w 1 = w 2 = w 3
questions.solution: Solution Gravitational force is a conservative force. For conservative forces, work done is path-independent — it depends only on the initial and final positions (A and B), not on the path taken. Since all three paths connect the same points A and B: w 1 = w 2 = w 3 Answer: (2) w 1 = w 2 = w 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Gravitational force is a conservative force.

For conservative forces, work done is path-independent — it depends only on the initial and final positions (A and B), not on the path taken.

Since all three paths connect the same points A and B:
w1=w2=w3

Answer: (2) w1=w2=w3

questions.solution: Solution Turned by 90 ° = π 2 rad after travelling 628 m. Arc length = r θ ⇒ r = 628 π / 2 = 628 × 2 π = 1256 3.14 = 400 m F c = m v 2 r = 1000 × 16 2 400 = 1000 × 256 400 = 640 N Answer: (3) 640 N

Turned by 90°=π2 rad after travelling 628 m.

Arc length =rθ ⇒ r=628π/2=628×2π=12563.14=400 m

Fc=mv2r=1000×162400=1000×256400=640 N

Answer: (3) 640 N

questions.solutionText: Solution Effective height: h = 550 − 50 = 500 m Input power = m g h t = 2000 × 10 × 500 1 = 10 7 W = 10 MW Output power = 80 % of 10 MW = 8 MW Answer: (1) 8 MW

Solution
Effective height: h=550−50=500 m

Input power =mght=2000×10×5001=107 W =10 MW

Output power =80% of 10 MW =8 MW

Answer: (1) 8 MW

The wavelength of each photon = 550 × 10 - 9 m The energy of each photon, E = hc λ = 6 . 6 3 × 1 0 - 3 4 × 3 × 1 0 8 5 5 0 × 1 0 - 9 = 3 . 6 1 6 × 1 0 - 1 9 J Number of photons incident on the earth's surface n = P E = 1 . 3 8 8 × 1 0 3 3 . 6 1 6 × 1 0 - 1 9 = 3 . 8 3 8 × 1 0 2 1 = 3 . 838 × 10 21 photon m - 2 s - 1
questions.solution: Solution Given, energy per unit area per second, p = 1 . 388 × 10 3 W m - 2 Let n be the number of photons incident on the earth per square metre. The wavelength of each photon = 550 × 10 - 9 m The energy of each photon, E = hc λ = 6 . 6 3 × 1 0 - 3 4 × 3 × 1 0 8 5 5 0 × 1 0 - 9 = 3 . 6 1 6 × 1 0 - 1 9 J Number of photons incident on the earth's surface n = P E = 1 . 3 8 8 × 1 0 3 3 . 6 1 6 × 1 0 - 1 9 = 3 . 8 3 8 × 1 0 2 1 = 3 . 838 × 10 21 photon m - 2 s - 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Given, energy per unit area per second, p=1.388×103 W m-2

Let n be the number of photons incident on the earth per square metre. The wavelength of each photon = 550×10-9 m

The energy of each photon, E=hcλ

=6.63×10-34×3×108550×10-9=3.616×10-19 J

Number of photons incident on the earth's surface

n=PE=1.388×1033.616×10-19=3.838×1021

=3.838×1021 photon m-2 s-1

questions.html: A square in a horizontal orientation is situated in a uniform horizontal electric field such that a line drawn in the plane of square makes angle of 300 with electric field. If side of square is a then flux through the square will be
questions.questionHtml: A square in a horizontal orientation is situated in a uniform horizontal electric field such that a line drawn in the plane of square makes angle of 300 with electric field. If side of square is a then flux through the square will be
questions.solutionText: Solution The angle between the electric field and the normal drawn to the plane of square is 90 ° . This means the angle between the electric field vector and the area vector is 90 ° . We know that the flux is the dot product of field and the area. Hence, as cos 90 ° is zero, the total flux will be zero.

Solution

The angle between the electric field and the normal drawn to the plane of square is 90°. This means the angle between the electric field vector and the area vector is 90°. We know that the flux is the dot product of field and the area. Hence, as cos90° is zero, the total flux will be zero.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The secondary pollutants formed in photochemical smog are
questions.questionText: The secondary pollutants formed in photochemical smog are
questions.question: The secondary pollutants formed in photochemical smog are
questions.prompt: The secondary pollutants formed in photochemical smog are
questions.html: The secondary pollutants formed in photochemical smog are
questions.questionHtml: The secondary pollutants formed in photochemical smog are
questions.solutionText: Solution Photochemical smog is formed by the action of sunlight on primary pollutants (NO, hydrocarbons). Primary pollutants: NO, unburnt hydrocarbons (directly emitted) These react in sunlight to form secondary pollutants: NO → NO 2 → reacts further to form O 3 (ozone) Hydrocarbons + NO 2 + sunlight → PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate) SO 2 and NO 2 are primary pollutants (directly emitted from combustion), not secondary. Hydrocarbons are primary pollutants. O 3 and PAN are formed in the atmosphere via photochemical reactions ⇒ secondary pollutants. Answer: (4) O 3 and PAN
questions.solution: Solution Photochemical smog is formed by the action of sunlight on primary pollutants (NO, hydrocarbons). Primary pollutants: NO, unburnt hydrocarbons (directly emitted) These react in sunlight to form secondary pollutants: NO → NO 2 → reacts further to form O 3 (ozone) Hydrocarbons + NO 2 + sunlight → PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate) SO 2 and NO 2 are primary pollutants (directly emitted from combustion), not secondary. Hydrocarbons are primary pollutants. O 3 and PAN are formed in the atmosphere via photochemical reactions ⇒ secondary pollutants. Answer: (4) O 3 and PAN
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Photochemical smog is formed by the action of sunlight on primary pollutants (NO, hydrocarbons).

Primary pollutants: NO, unburnt hydrocarbons (directly emitted)

These react in sunlight to form secondary pollutants:
NO → NO2 → reacts further to form O3 (ozone)
Hydrocarbons + NO2 + sunlight → PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)

SO2 and NO2 are primary pollutants (directly emitted from combustion), not secondary.
Hydrocarbons are primary pollutants.
O3 and PAN are formed in the atmosphere via photochemical reactions ⇒ secondary pollutants.

Answer: (4) O3 and PAN

questions.html: Which of the following lead oxides is present in 'Sindhur'?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following lead oxides is present in 'Sindhur'?
questions.solutionText: Solution Sindhur (also called Sindoor) is the traditional red/orange powder used in India. It is chemically red lead, also known as minium, which has the formula P b 3 O 4 . P b 3 O 4 is a mixed oxide of lead containing both P b 2 + and P b 4 + (i.e., 2 P b O ⋅ P b O 2 ). Answer: (4) P b 3 O 4
questions.solution: Solution Sindhur (also called Sindoor) is the traditional red/orange powder used in India. It is chemically red lead, also known as minium, which has the formula P b 3 O 4 . P b 3 O 4 is a mixed oxide of lead containing both P b 2 + and P b 4 + (i.e., 2 P b O ⋅ P b O 2 ). Answer: (4) P b 3 O 4
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Sindhur (also called Sindoor) is the traditional red/orange powder used in India.

It is chemically red lead, also known as minium, which has the formula Pb3O4.

Pb3O4 is a mixed oxide of lead containing both Pb2+ and Pb4+ (i.e., 2PbO⋅PbO2).

Answer: (4) Pb3O4

questions.questionHtml:
An aromatic compound 'X' with molecular formula C9H10O gives the following chemical tests
1. Forms 2, 4-DNP derivative,
2. Reduces Tollen's reagent,
3. Undergoes Cannizaro reaction and,
4. On vigorous oxidation 1, 2-benzenedicarboxylic acid is obtained.

Compound X is
questions.solutionText: Solution 1. X forms 2, 4-DNP derivatives, it shows that it is a carbonyl compound (C=0). 2. It reduces Tollen's reagent, it shows that it has an aldehyde group. 3. It undergoes Cannizaro reaction, that also shows the presence of an aldehyde having no α -hydrogen. 4. On vigorous oxidation, it produces 1, 2-benzenedicarboxylic acid. It shows that groups are present at 1,2-position on benzene ring. Thus, the correct structure of the compound X is
questions.solution: Solution 1. X forms 2, 4-DNP derivatives, it shows that it is a carbonyl compound (C=0). 2. It reduces Tollen's reagent, it shows that it has an aldehyde group. 3. It undergoes Cannizaro reaction, that also shows the presence of an aldehyde having no α -hydrogen. 4. On vigorous oxidation, it produces 1, 2-benzenedicarboxylic acid. It shows that groups are present at 1,2-position on benzene ring. Thus, the correct structure of the compound X is
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
1. X forms 2, 4-DNP derivatives, it shows that it is a carbonyl compound (C=0).
2. It reduces Tollen's reagent, it shows that it has an aldehyde group.
3. It undergoes Cannizaro reaction, that also shows the presence of an aldehyde having no α-hydrogen.
4. On vigorous oxidation, it produces 1, 2-benzenedicarboxylic acid. It shows that groups are present at 1,2-position on benzene ring.
Thus, the correct structure of the compound X is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding the methyl free radical ( CH 3 ˙ ) ?
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding the methyl free radical ( CH 3 ˙ ) ?
questions.question: Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding the methyl free radical ( CH 3 ˙ ) ?
questions.prompt: Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding the methyl free radical ( CH 3 ˙ ) ?
questions.html: Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding the methyl free radical (CH3˙) ?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding the methyl free radical (CH3˙) ?
questions.solutionText: Solution Methyl free radical C ˙ H 3 has 1 unpaired electron on carbon. A. Carbon has 3 bond pairs + 1 unpaired electron ⇒ s p 2 hybridized. True. B. s p 2 hybridization ⇒ planar geometry, bond angles ≈ 120 ° . True. C. It has 1 unpaired electron ⇒ it is paramagnetic, NOT diamagnetic. This is incorrect. D. Free radicals are electrically neutral (no charge). True. Answer: C) It is diamagnetic in nature due to the presence of an odd electron.
questions.solution: Solution Methyl free radical C ˙ H 3 has 1 unpaired electron on carbon. A. Carbon has 3 bond pairs + 1 unpaired electron ⇒ s p 2 hybridized. True. B. s p 2 hybridization ⇒ planar geometry, bond angles ≈ 120 ° . True. C. It has 1 unpaired electron ⇒ it is paramagnetic, NOT diamagnetic. This is incorrect. D. Free radicals are electrically neutral (no charge). True. Answer: C) It is diamagnetic in nature due to the presence of an odd electron.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Methyl free radical C˙H3 has 1 unpaired electron on carbon.

A. Carbon has 3 bond pairs + 1 unpaired electron ⇒ sp2 hybridized. True.

B. sp2 hybridization ⇒ planar geometry, bond angles ≈120°. True.

C. It has 1 unpaired electron ⇒ it is paramagnetic, NOT diamagnetic. This is incorrect.

D. Free radicals are electrically neutral (no charge). True.

Answer: C) It is diamagnetic in nature due to the presence of an odd electron.

questions.solutionText: Solution A primary pollutant is an air pollutant emitted directly from a source and a secondary pollutant is not directly emitted, but forms when other pollutants (primary pollutants) react in the atmosphere. SO 2 , NO 2 and NO are primary pollutants while H 2 SO 4 is secondary pollutant as it is not directly released into the atmosphere.

questions.text: 99 % of a first order reaction was completed in 32 min . When will 99.9 % of the reaction complete?

questions.prompt: 99 % of a first order reaction was completed in 32 min . When will 99.9 % of the reaction complete?
questions.html: 99% of a first order reaction was completed in 32 min . When will 99.9% of the reaction complete?
questions.questionHtml: 99% of a first order reaction was completed in 32 min . When will 99.9% of the reaction complete?
questions.solutionText: Solution For first order reaction: t = 2.303 k log ⁡ 100 100 − % completion For 99% completion: 32 = 2.303 k log ⁡ 100 1 = 2.303 × 2 k k = 2.303 × 2 32 For 99.9% completion: t = 2.303 k log ⁡ 100 0.1 = 2.303 × 3 k t 32 = 3 2 ⇒ t = 48 min Answer: (4) 48 min
questions.solution: Solution For first order reaction: t = 2.303 k log ⁡ 100 100 − % completion For 99% completion: 32 = 2.303 k log ⁡ 100 1 = 2.303 × 2 k k = 2.303 × 2 32 For 99.9% completion: t = 2.303 k log ⁡ 100 0.1 = 2.303 × 3 k t 32 = 3 2 ⇒ t = 48 min Answer: (4) 48 min
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For first order reaction: t=2.303klog⁡100100−% completion

For 99% completion:
32=2.303klog⁡1001=2.303×2k
k=2.303×232

For 99.9% completion:
t=2.303klog⁡1000.1=2.303×3k

t32=32 ⇒ t=48 min

Answer: (4) 48 min

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Compound which gives acetone on ozonolysis
questions.questionText: Compound which gives acetone on ozonolysis
questions.question: Compound which gives acetone on ozonolysis
questions.prompt: Compound which gives acetone on ozonolysis
questions.html: Compound which gives acetone on ozonolysis
questions.questionHtml: Compound which gives acetone on ozonolysis
questions.solutionText: Solution Ozonolysis cleaves the double bond and gives carbonyl compounds from each fragment. For acetone ( C H 3 C O C H 3 ) to be produced, both carbons of the double bond must each have two methyl groups attached. ( C H 3 ) 2 C = C ( C H 3 ) 2 : each carbon of the double bond has two C H 3 groups ⇒ ozonolysis gives C H 3 C O C H 3 + C H 3 C O C H 3 (two moles of acetone). (1) C H 3 − C H = C H − C H 3 ⇒ gives C H 3 C H O (acetaldehyde) (3) C 6 H 5 C H = C H 2 ⇒ gives C 6 H 5 C H O and H C H O (4) C H 3 C H = C H 2 ⇒ gives C H 3 C H O and H C H O Answer: (2) ( C H 3 ) 2 C = C ( C H 3 ) 2
questions.solution: Solution Ozonolysis cleaves the double bond and gives carbonyl compounds from each fragment. For acetone ( C H 3 C O C H 3 ) to be produced, both carbons of the double bond must each have two methyl groups attached. ( C H 3 ) 2 C = C ( C H 3 ) 2 : each carbon of the double bond has two C H 3 groups ⇒ ozonolysis gives C H 3 C O C H 3 + C H 3 C O C H 3 (two moles of acetone). (1) C H 3 − C H = C H − C H 3 ⇒ gives C H 3 C H O (acetaldehyde) (3) C 6 H 5 C H = C H 2 ⇒ gives C 6 H 5 C H O and H C H O (4) C H 3 C H = C H 2 ⇒ gives C H 3 C H O and H C H O Answer: (2) ( C H 3 ) 2 C = C ( C H 3 ) 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Ozonolysis cleaves the double bond and gives carbonyl compounds from each fragment.

For acetone (CH3COCH3) to be produced, both carbons of the double bond must each have two methyl groups attached.

(CH3)2C=C(CH3)2: each carbon of the double bond has two CH3 groups ⇒ ozonolysis gives CH3COCH3 + CH3COCH3 (two moles of acetone).

(1) CH3−CH=CH−CH3 ⇒ gives CH3CHO (acetaldehyde)
(3) C6H5CH=CH2 ⇒ gives C6H5CHO and HCHO
(4) CH3CH=CH2 ⇒ gives CH3CHO and HCHO

Answer: (2) (CH3)2C=C(CH3)2

questions.text: What is the correct decreasing order for acid strength?
questions.questionText: What is the correct decreasing order for acid strength?
questions.question: What is the correct decreasing order for acid strength?
questions.prompt: What is the correct decreasing order for acid strength?
questions.html: What is the correct decreasing order for acid strength?
questions.questionHtml: What is the correct decreasing order for acid strength?
questions.solutionText: Solution Acid strength increases with electron-withdrawing ability of substituent ( − I effect stabilises carboxylate). Order of − I effect: N O 2 > C N > F > C l ⇒ N O 2 C H 2 C O O H > N C C H 2 C O O H > F C H 2 C O O H > C l C H 2 C O O H Answer: (2)
questions.solution: Solution Acid strength increases with electron-withdrawing ability of substituent ( − I effect stabilises carboxylate). Order of − I effect: N O 2 > C N > F > C l ⇒ N O 2 C H 2 C O O H > N C C H 2 C O O H > F C H 2 C O O H > C l C H 2 C O O H Answer: (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Acid strength increases with electron-withdrawing ability of substituent (−I effect stabilises carboxylate).

Order of −I effect: NO2>CN>F>Cl

⇒ NO2CH2COOH>NCCH2COOH>FCH2COOH>ClCH2COOH

Answer: (2)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Glucose and fructose form
questions.questionText: Glucose and fructose form
questions.question: Glucose and fructose form
questions.prompt: Glucose and fructose form
questions.html: Glucose and fructose form
questions.questionHtml: Glucose and fructose form
questions.solutionText: Solution Glucose and fructose are epimers that differ in configuration at C-1 and C-2. Osazone formation involves reaction with phenylhydrazine at C-1 and C-2, destroying the difference between the two. Since both have identical configurations at C-3, C-4, C-5, and C-6, they form the same osazone (glucosazone). On oxidation → glucose gives gluconic/saccharic acid, fructose gives different acids. Not the same. On reduction → glucose gives sorbitol, fructose gives mannitol (different). Not the same. Answer: (1) Same osazone
questions.solution: Solution Glucose and fructose are epimers that differ in configuration at C-1 and C-2. Osazone formation involves reaction with phenylhydrazine at C-1 and C-2, destroying the difference between the two. Since both have identical configurations at C-3, C-4, C-5, and C-6, they form the same osazone (glucosazone). On oxidation → glucose gives gluconic/saccharic acid, fructose gives different acids. Not the same. On reduction → glucose gives sorbitol, fructose gives mannitol (different). Not the same. Answer: (1) Same osazone
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Glucose and fructose are epimers that differ in configuration at C-1 and C-2.

Osazone formation involves reaction with phenylhydrazine at C-1 and C-2, destroying the difference between the two. Since both have identical configurations at C-3, C-4, C-5, and C-6, they form the same osazone (glucosazone).

On oxidation → glucose gives gluconic/saccharic acid, fructose gives different acids. Not the same.
On reduction → glucose gives sorbitol, fructose gives mannitol (different). Not the same.

Answer: (1) Same osazone

questions.text: When N 2 goes to N 2 + , the N − N bond distance ..... and when O 2 goes to O 2 + , the O − O bond distance .......
questions.questionText: When N 2 goes to N 2 + , the N − N bond distance ..... and when O 2 goes to O 2 + , the O − O bond distance .......

questions.html: When N2 goes to N2+, the N−N bond distance ..... and when O2 goes to O2+, the O−O bond distance .......
questions.questionHtml: When N2 goes to N2+, the N−N bond distance ..... and when O2 goes to O2+, the O−O bond distance .......
questions.solutionText: Solution When N 2 goes to N 2 + , the N − N bond distance increase, and when O 2 goes to O 2 + , the O − O bond distance decrease. The bond order of the nitrogen molecule is 3 . An electron from bonding π Molecular Orbital is lost to form N 2 + . The bond order decreases to 2.5 . This decreases the strength of the bond and the bond distance increase. The bond order of oxygen is 2 . An electron is lost from antibonding π Molecular Orbital to form O 2 + . The bond order increases to 2.5 . This increases the strength of the bond and the bond distance decreases.
questions.solution: Solution When N 2 goes to N 2 + , the N − N bond distance increase, and when O 2 goes to O 2 + , the O − O bond distance decrease. The bond order of the nitrogen molecule is 3 . An electron from bonding π Molecular Orbital is lost to form N 2 + . The bond order decreases to 2.5 . This decreases the strength of the bond and the bond distance increase. The bond order of oxygen is 2 . An electron is lost from antibonding π Molecular Orbital to form O 2 + . The bond order increases to 2.5 . This increases the strength of the bond and the bond distance decreases.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
When N2 goes to N2+, the N−N bond distance increase, and when O2 goes to O2+, the O−O bond distance decrease.
The bond order of the nitrogen molecule is 3 . An electron from bonding π Molecular Orbital is lost to form N2+. The bond order decreases to 2.5.
This decreases the strength of the bond and the bond distance increase.
The bond order of oxygen is 2 . An electron is lost from antibonding π Molecular Orbital to form O2+. The bond order increases to 2.5. This increases the strength of the bond and the bond distance decreases.

questions.html: An ideal gas expands isothermally and reversibly from 10 m3 to 20 m3 at 300 K, performing 5.187 kJ of work on surrounding, calculate number of moles of gas used?
questions.questionHtml: An ideal gas expands isothermally and reversibly from 10 m3 to 20 m3 at 300 K, performing 5.187 kJ of work on surrounding, calculate number of moles of gas used?
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
V1=10 m3=10×103dm3V2=20 m3=20×103dm3
T=300K,W=−5.187 kJ=−5187 J
W=−2.303nRTlog⁡V2V1
∴−5187=−2.303×n×8.314×300×log10⁡20×10310×103
∴n=51872.303×8.314×300×0.301
∴n=3 moles

questions.text: Which among the following salts turns blue litmus red in its aqueous solution?
questions.questionText: Which among the following salts turns blue litmus red in its aqueous solution?

questions.prompt: Which among the following salts turns blue litmus red in its aqueous solution?
questions.html: Which among the following salts turns blue litmus red in its aqueous solution?
questions.questionHtml: Which among the following salts turns blue litmus red in its aqueous solution?
questions.solutionText: Solution In acidic solution blue litmus paper turns red. 1. NH 4 CN → Basic solution 2. NH 4 F is a salt of weak acid HF ( K a = 7.2 × 10 − 4 ) and weak base NH 4 OH ( K b = 1.8 × 10 − 5 ) . Since K a > K b , the solution of NH 4 F will be acidic in nature. 3. CH 3 COONa → Basic solution 4. CH 3 COONH 4 → Neutral solution.
questions.solution: Solution In acidic solution blue litmus paper turns red. 1. NH 4 CN → Basic solution 2. NH 4 F is a salt of weak acid HF ( K a = 7.2 × 10 − 4 ) and weak base NH 4 OH ( K b = 1.8 × 10 − 5 ) . Since K a > K b , the solution of NH 4 F will be acidic in nature. 3. CH 3 COONa → Basic solution 4. CH 3 COONH 4 → Neutral solution.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In acidic solution blue litmus paper turns red.
1. NH4CN→ Basic solution
2. NH4 F is a salt of weak acid HF(Ka=7.2×10−4) and weak base NH4OH(Kb=1.8×10−5). Since Ka>Kb, the solution of NH4 F will be acidic in nature.
3.CH3COONa→ Basic solution
4.CH3COONH4→ Neutral solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which is an electrophile
questions.questionText: Which is an electrophile
questions.question: Which is an electrophile
questions.prompt: Which is an electrophile
questions.html: Which is an electrophile
questions.questionHtml: Which is an electrophile
questions.solutionText: Solution Electrophiles are electron-deficient species that accept electron pairs. B C l 3 : boron has only 6 electrons (incomplete octet), strong electron acceptor ⇒ electrophile. C H 3 O H : neutral, lone pairs on O make it nucleophilic. N H 3 : lone pair on N ⇒ nucleophile. A l C l 4 − : has negative charge, complete octet ⇒ not electrophilic. Answer: (1) B C l 3
questions.solution: Solution Electrophiles are electron-deficient species that accept electron pairs. B C l 3 : boron has only 6 electrons (incomplete octet), strong electron acceptor ⇒ electrophile. C H 3 O H : neutral, lone pairs on O make it nucleophilic. N H 3 : lone pair on N ⇒ nucleophile. A l C l 4 − : has negative charge, complete octet ⇒ not electrophilic. Answer: (1) B C l 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Electrophiles are electron-deficient species that accept electron pairs.

BCl3: boron has only 6 electrons (incomplete octet), strong electron acceptor ⇒ electrophile.
CH3OH: neutral, lone pairs on O make it nucleophilic.
NH3: lone pair on N ⇒ nucleophile.
AlCl4−: has negative charge, complete octet ⇒ not electrophilic.

Answer: (1) BCl3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is used as refrigerant?
questions.questionText: Which of the following is used as refrigerant?
questions.question: Which of the following is used as refrigerant?
questions.prompt: Which of the following is used as refrigerant?
questions.html: Which of the following is used as refrigerant?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following is used as refrigerant?
questions.solutionText: Solution Refrigerants must be easily liquefiable, non-toxic, non-corrosive, and have low boiling points. SiC → silicon carbide, an abrasive/ceramic material, not a refrigerant. SO 2 → toxic, corrosive, used in older refrigeration but not preferred. CHCl 3 → chloroform, used as solvent/anaesthetic, not a refrigerant. CF 2 Cl 2 → Freon-12 (dichlorodifluoromethane), a mixed halide of fluorine and chlorine, easily liquefiable, low boiling point, widely used as a refrigerant. Answer: (3) C F 2 C l 2
questions.solution: Solution Refrigerants must be easily liquefiable, non-toxic, non-corrosive, and have low boiling points. SiC → silicon carbide, an abrasive/ceramic material, not a refrigerant. SO 2 → toxic, corrosive, used in older refrigeration but not preferred. CHCl 3 → chloroform, used as solvent/anaesthetic, not a refrigerant. CF 2 Cl 2 → Freon-12 (dichlorodifluoromethane), a mixed halide of fluorine and chlorine, easily liquefiable, low boiling point, widely used as a refrigerant. Answer: (3) C F 2 C l 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Refrigerants must be easily liquefiable, non-toxic, non-corrosive, and have low boiling points.

SiC → silicon carbide, an abrasive/ceramic material, not a refrigerant.
SO2 → toxic, corrosive, used in older refrigeration but not preferred.
CHCl3 → chloroform, used as solvent/anaesthetic, not a refrigerant.
CF2Cl2 → Freon-12 (dichlorodifluoromethane), a mixed halide of fluorine and chlorine, easily liquefiable, low boiling point, widely used as a refrigerant.

Answer: (3) CF2Cl2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which one of the following statements regarding Henry's law is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements regarding Henry's law is not correct?
questions.question: Which one of the following statements regarding Henry's law is not correct?
questions.prompt: Which one of the following statements regarding Henry's law is not correct?
questions.html: Which one of the following statements regarding Henry's law is not correct?
questions.questionHtml: Which one of the following statements regarding Henry's law is not correct?
questions.solutionText: Solution Henry's law: p = K H ⋅ x , where p is partial pressure and x is mole fraction of gas in solution. (1) K H depends on the nature of the gas. True. (2) From p = K H ⋅ x ⇒ x = p K H . Higher K H means lower solubility at a given pressure. So this statement is incorrect. (3) Partial pressure ∝ mole fraction. This is exactly Henry's law. True. (4) Different gases have different K H values. True. Answer: (2)
questions.solution: Solution Henry's law: p = K H ⋅ x , where p is partial pressure and x is mole fraction of gas in solution. (1) K H depends on the nature of the gas. True. (2) From p = K H ⋅ x ⇒ x = p K H . Higher K H means lower solubility at a given pressure. So this statement is incorrect. (3) Partial pressure ∝ mole fraction. This is exactly Henry's law. True. (4) Different gases have different K H values. True. Answer: (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Henry's law: p=KH⋅x, where p is partial pressure and x is mole fraction of gas in solution.

(1) KH depends on the nature of the gas. True.

(2) From p=KH⋅x ⇒ x=pKH. Higher KH means lower solubility at a given pressure. So this statement is incorrect.

(3) Partial pressure ∝ mole fraction. This is exactly Henry's law. True.

(4) Different gases have different KH values. True.

Answer: (2)

questions.text: Ethyl amine on heating with CS 2 in presence of HgCl 2 forms
questions.questionText: Ethyl amine on heating with CS 2 in presence of HgCl 2 forms
questions.question: Ethyl amine on heating with CS 2 in presence of HgCl 2 forms
questions.prompt: Ethyl amine on heating with CS 2 in presence of HgCl 2 forms
questions.html: Ethyl amine on heating with CS2 in presence of HgCl2 forms
questions.questionHtml: Ethyl amine on heating with CS2 in presence of HgCl2 forms
questions.solutionText: Solution Reaction of primary amine with C S 2 in presence of H g C l 2 : C 2 H 5 N H 2 + C S 2 → H g C l 2 , Δ C 2 H 5 N C S (ethyl isothiocyanate) + H 2 S The mechanism: amine first reacts with C S 2 to form a dithiocarbamic acid salt, which on heating with H g C l 2 (which removes H 2 S ) gives isothiocyanate ( − N C S ). Answer: (1) C 2 H 5 N C S
questions.solution: Solution Reaction of primary amine with C S 2 in presence of H g C l 2 : C 2 H 5 N H 2 + C S 2 → H g C l 2 , Δ C 2 H 5 N C S (ethyl isothiocyanate) + H 2 S The mechanism: amine first reacts with C S 2 to form a dithiocarbamic acid salt, which on heating with H g C l 2 (which removes H 2 S ) gives isothiocyanate ( − N C S ). Answer: (1) C 2 H 5 N C S
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Reaction of primary amine with CS2 in presence of HgCl2:

C2H5NH2+CS2→HgCl2,Δ C2H5NCS (ethyl isothiocyanate) +H2S

The mechanism: amine first reacts with CS2 to form a dithiocarbamic acid salt, which on heating with HgCl2 (which removes H2S) gives isothiocyanate (−NCS).

Answer: (1) C2H5NCS

questions.prompt: The Structural formula of an amino acid, isoleucine is
questions.html: The Structural formula of an amino acid, isoleucine is
questions.questionHtml: The Structural formula of an amino acid, isoleucine is
questions.solutionText: Solution Isoleucine is a branched-chain essential amino acid with molecular formula C 6 H 13 N O 2 . Its IUPAC name is 2-amino-3-methylpentanoic acid, meaning: 5-carbon main chain, methyl branch at C-3, amino group at C-2. This corresponds to a structure with C 2 H 5 and C H 3 groups on the same carbon, attached to C H − C H . C O O H with N H 2 on the α -carbon. Answer: (3)
questions.solution: Solution Isoleucine is a branched-chain essential amino acid with molecular formula C 6 H 13 N O 2 . Its IUPAC name is 2-amino-3-methylpentanoic acid, meaning: 5-carbon main chain, methyl branch at C-3, amino group at C-2. This corresponds to a structure with C 2 H 5 and C H 3 groups on the same carbon, attached to C H − C H . C O O H with N H 2 on the α -carbon. Answer: (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Isoleucine is a branched-chain essential amino acid with molecular formula C6H13NO2.

Its IUPAC name is 2-amino-3-methylpentanoic acid, meaning:
5-carbon main chain, methyl branch at C-3, amino group at C-2.

This corresponds to a structure with C2H5 and CH3 groups on the same carbon, attached to CH−CH.COOH with NH2 on the α-carbon.

Answer: (3)

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Paragraph The complexity of modern problems often precludes any one person from fully understanding them. Factors contributing to rising obesity levels, for example, include transportation systems and infrastructure, media, convenience foods, changing social norms, human biology and psychological factors.... The multidimensional or layered character of complex problems also undermines the principle of meritocracy: the idea that the ‘best person’ should be hired. There is no best person. When putting together an oncological research team, a biotech company such as Gilead or Genentech would not construct a multiple-choice test and hire the top scorers, or hire people whose resumes score highest according to some performance criteria. Instead, they would seek diversity. They would build a team of people who bring diverse knowledge bases, tools and analytic skills.... Believers in a meritocracy might grant that teams ought to be diverse but then argue that meritocratic principles should apply within each category. Thus the team should consist of the ‘best’ mathematicians, the ‘best’ oncologists, and the ‘best’ biostatisticians from within the pool. That position suffers from a similar flaw. Even with a knowledge domain, no test or criteria applied to individuals will produce the best team. Each of these domains possesses such depth and breadth, that no test can exist. Consider the field of neuroscience. Upwards of 50,000 papers were published last year covering various techniques, domains of enquiry and levels of analysis, ranging from molecules and synapses up through networks of neurons. Given that complexity, any attempt to rank a collection of neuroscientists from best to worst, as if they were competitors in the 50-metre butterfly, must fail. What could be true is that given a specific task and the composition of a particular team, one scientist would be more likely to contribute than another. Optimal hiring depends on context. Optimal teams will be diverse. Evidence for this claim can be seen in the way that papers and patents that combine diverse ideas tend to rank as high-impact. It can also be found in the structure of the so-called random decision forest, a state-of-the-art machine-learning algorithm. Random forests consist of ensembles of decision trees. If classifying pictures, each tree makes a vote: is that a picture of a fox or a dog? A weighted majority rules. Random forests can serve many ends. They can identify bank fraud and diseases, recommend ceiling fans and predict online dating behaviour. When building a forest, you do not select the best trees as they tend to make similar classifications. You want diversity. Programmers achieve that diversity by training each tree on different data, a technique known as bagging. They also boost the forest ‘cognitively’ by training trees on the hardest cases – those that the current forest gets wrong. This ensures even more diversity and accurate forests. Yet the fallacy of meritocracy persists. Corporations, non-profits, governments, universities and even preschools test, score and hire the ‘best’. This all but guarantees not creating the best team. Ranking people by common criteria produces homogeneity.... That’s not likely to lead to breakthroughs. Question Which of the following conditions would weaken the efficacy of a random decision forest?

questions.prompt: Paragraph The complexity of modern problems often precludes any one person from fully understanding them. Factors contributing to rising obesity levels, for example, include transportation systems and infrastructure, media, convenience foods, changing social norms, human biology and psychological factors.... The multidimensional or layered character of complex problems also undermines the principle of meritocracy: the idea that the ‘best person’ should be hired. There is no best person. When putting together an oncological research team, a biotech company such as Gilead or Genentech would not construct a multiple-choice test and hire the top scorers, or hire people whose resumes score highest according to some performance criteria. Instead, they would seek diversity. They would build a team of people who bring diverse knowledge bases, tools and analytic skills.... Believers in a meritocracy might grant that teams ought to be diverse but then argue that meritocratic principles should apply within each category. Thus the team should consist of the ‘best’ mathematicians, the ‘best’ oncologists, and the ‘best’ biostatisticians from within the pool. That position suffers from a similar flaw. Even with a knowledge domain, no test or criteria applied to individuals will produce the best team. Each of these domains possesses such depth and breadth, that no test can exist. Consider the field of neuroscience. Upwards of 50,000 papers were published last year covering various techniques, domains of enquiry and levels of analysis, ranging from molecules and synapses up through networks of neurons. Given that complexity, any attempt to rank a collection of neuroscientists from best to worst, as if they were competitors in the 50-metre butterfly, must fail. What could be true is that given a specific task and the composition of a particular team, one scientist would be more likely to contribute than another. Optimal hiring depends on context. Optimal teams will be diverse. Evidence for this claim can be seen in the way that papers and patents that combine diverse ideas tend to rank as high-impact. It can also be found in the structure of the so-called random decision forest, a state-of-the-art machine-learning algorithm. Random forests consist of ensembles of decision trees. If classifying pictures, each tree makes a vote: is that a picture of a fox or a dog? A weighted majority rules. Random forests can serve many ends. They can identify bank fraud and diseases, recommend ceiling fans and predict online dating behaviour. When building a forest, you do not select the best trees as they tend to make similar classifications. You want diversity. Programmers achieve that diversity by training each tree on different data, a technique known as bagging. They also boost the forest ‘cognitively’ by training trees on the hardest cases – those that the current forest gets wrong. This ensures even more diversity and accurate forests. Yet the fallacy of meritocracy persists. Corporations, non-profits, governments, universities and even preschools test, score and hire the ‘best’. This all but guarantees not creating the best team. Ranking people by common criteria produces homogeneity.... That’s not likely to lead to breakthroughs. Question Which of the following conditions would weaken the efficacy of a random decision forest?

questions.solutionText: Solution The last sentence of the second last para says: Programmers also boost the forest ?cognitively? by training trees on the hardest cases ? those that the current forest gets wrong. This ensures even more diversity and accurate forests. Thus, if we want to weaken the efficacy of a random decision forest, we should train a large number of decision trees on data derived from easy cases. Thus option 4 directly weakens the argument. There is no need to test the other choices.

Resolute → firmly determined (unrelated) Verbose → using too many words (unrelated) Rigid → stiff, inflexible (unrelated) Shrewd → having sharp powers of judgement (closest match) Answer: A) Shrewd

Resolute → firmly determined (unrelated)
Verbose → using too many words (unrelated)
Rigid → stiff, inflexible (unrelated)

Shrewd → having sharp powers of judgement (closest match)

Answer: A) Shrewd

questions.solutionText: Solution Appeasement means the action of giving in to demands in order to maintain peace or avoid conflict. Proliferation → rapid increase/spread (unrelated) Rectification → correction of errors (unrelated) Abnegation → self-denial (unrelated) Conciliation → the act of making peace, placating, or reconciling parties (closest match) Answer: (2) Conciliation

Conciliation → the act of making peace, placating, or reconciling parties (closest match)

Answer: (2) Conciliation

questions.solutionText: Solution The correct preposition after "comply" is "with" — one complies with something (a rule, pressure, demand). "Comply under/to/for" are all incorrect collocations in standard English usage. Answer: (3) with

"Comply under/to/for" are all incorrect collocations in standard English usage.

Answer: (3) with

questions.solutionText: Solution The appropriate word to be used in the blank is 'assured.' The term 'assured' is used to give positive expectation or guarantee. Here, the assurance of getting passed is inferred in the given sentence. 'Insured' means to be covered by insurance. 'Ensured' is used to make certain of providing or obtaining something. 'Assumed' means to accept something to be true without question or proof.

'Assumed' means to accept something to be true without question or proof.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

The appropriate word to be used in the blank is 'assured.' The term 'assured' is used to give positive expectation or guarantee. Here, the assurance of getting passed is inferred in the given sentence.

'Insured' means to be covered by insurance.
'Ensured' is used to make certain of providing or obtaining something.
'Assumed' means to accept something to be true without question or proof.

questions.text: Read the following passage carefully and answer the question that follows, focusing on the contextual meaning of the highlighted idiom. The board meeting was tense as the company faced a significant deficit. While the junior analysts proposed minor budget cuts, the CEO insisted that these were merely "band-aid solutions." He argued that unless they addressed the core inefficiency in their supply chain, they were simply kicking the can down the road. The investors, however, were more interested in short-term dividends than long-term structural changes. Question In the context of the passage, what does the idiom "kicking the can down the road" most nearly mean?

questions.questionHtml:
Read the following passage carefully and answer the question that follows, focusing on the contextual meaning of the highlighted idiom.

The board meeting was tense as the company faced a significant deficit. While the junior analysts proposed minor budget cuts, the CEO insisted that these were merely "band-aid solutions." He argued that unless they addressed the core inefficiency in their supply chain, they were simply kicking the can down the road. The investors, however, were more interested in short-term dividends than long-term structural changes.

Question
In the context of the passage, what does the idiom "kicking the can down the road" most nearly mean?
questions.solutionText: Solution The CEO argues that minor budget cuts only avoid addressing the real problem (core supply chain inefficiency), implying the company is postponing a difficult but necessary decision. "Kicking the can down the road" is a well-known idiom meaning to defer or delay dealing with a problem rather than resolving it now. A → unrelated to the passage context C → no celebration is implied D → no mention of competition Answer: B) To delay making a difficult decision or dealing with a problem
questions.solution: Solution The CEO argues that minor budget cuts only avoid addressing the real problem (core supply chain inefficiency), implying the company is postponing a difficult but necessary decision. "Kicking the can down the road" is a well-known idiom meaning to defer or delay dealing with a problem rather than resolving it now. A → unrelated to the passage context C → no celebration is implied D → no mention of competition Answer: B) To delay making a difficult decision or dealing with a problem
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The CEO argues that minor budget cuts only avoid addressing the real problem (core supply chain inefficiency), implying the company is postponing a difficult but necessary decision.

"Kicking the can down the road" is a well-known idiom meaning to defer or delay dealing with a problem rather than resolving it now.

A → unrelated to the passage context
C → no celebration is implied
D → no mention of competition

Answer: B) To delay making a difficult decision or dealing with a problem

questions.solutionText: Solution The direction of the arrow visualizes the front view, as it is oriented parallel on the vertical plane, the established orthographic view shows the width and height of the object. The depth dimension does not show in the front view. Using the projection lines as references, the size and points between the related views will be horizontal or vertical lines in the front view. In the figure we can see an 'L' shaped figure at the front whereas we can not see it in option 1. In option 2 there is a slant line on the right hand side but there is no such line in the figure. According to the angle of view the tallest shape has to be on the right hand side but it is on the opposite side in option 3 Thus option 4 is the correct one.

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: A group of friends are sitting in an arrangement one each at the corner of an octagon. All are facing the centre. Mahima is sitting diagonally opposite Rama, who is on Sushma's right. Ravi is next to Sushma and opposite Girdhar. who is on Chandra's left. Savitri is not on Mahima's right but opposite Shalini. Who is on Shalini's right?

questions.questionHtml: A group of friends are sitting in an arrangement one each at the corner of an octagon. All are facing the centre. Mahima is sitting diagonally opposite Rama, who is on Sushma's right. Ravi is next to Sushma and opposite Girdhar. who is on Chandra's left. Savitri is not on Mahima's right but opposite Shalini. Who is on Shalini's right?
questions.solutionText: Solution Basic Structure: Since the friends are sitting at the corners of an octagon, we number the positions clockwise as P 1 , P 2 , P 3 , P 4 , P 5 , P 6 , P 7 , P 8 , all facing the center. Mahima and Rama: - Mahima is diagonally opposite Rama. - This means if Mahima is at position P 1 , Rama will be at P 5 (or any such diagonal pair). Rama and Sushma: - Rama is on Sushma's right. - Since everyone is facing the center, right means clockwise. - If Rama is at P 5 , then Sushma must be at P 4 . Ravi next to Sushma and opposite Girdhar: - Ravi is next to Sushma → He can be at P 3 or P 5 . - Ravi is opposite Girdhar → If Ravi is at P 3 , then Girdhar must be at P 7 . Girdhar is on Chandra's left: - If Girdhar is at P 7 , then Chandra must be at P 8 (since left means counterclockwise). Savitri and Shalini: - Savitri is not on Mahima's right. - Savitri is opposite Shalini. - If Mahima is at P 1 , Mahima's right (clockwise) is P 2 . - So, Savitri cannot be at P 2 . - The only remaining valid position for Savitri is P 6 , meaning Shalini is opposite at P 2 . Who is on Shalini's right? - Shalini is at P 2 , so Shalini's right (clockwise) is P 3 . - Ravi is at P 3 .
questions.solution: Solution Basic Structure: Since the friends are sitting at the corners of an octagon, we number the positions clockwise as P 1 , P 2 , P 3 , P 4 , P 5 , P 6 , P 7 , P 8 , all facing the center. Mahima and Rama: - Mahima is diagonally opposite Rama. - This means if Mahima is at position P 1 , Rama will be at P 5 (or any such diagonal pair). Rama and Sushma: - Rama is on Sushma's right. - Since everyone is facing the center, right means clockwise. - If Rama is at P 5 , then Sushma must be at P 4 . Ravi next to Sushma and opposite Girdhar: - Ravi is next to Sushma → He can be at P 3 or P 5 . - Ravi is opposite Girdhar → If Ravi is at P 3 , then Girdhar must be at P 7 . Girdhar is on Chandra's left: - If Girdhar is at P 7 , then Chandra must be at P 8 (since left means counterclockwise). Savitri and Shalini: - Savitri is not on Mahima's right. - Savitri is opposite Shalini. - If Mahima is at P 1 , Mahima's right (clockwise) is P 2 . - So, Savitri cannot be at P 2 . - The only remaining valid position for Savitri is P 6 , meaning Shalini is opposite at P 2 . Who is on Shalini's right? - Shalini is at P 2 , so Shalini's right (clockwise) is P 3 . - Ravi is at P 3 .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Basic Structure: Since the friends are sitting at the corners of an octagon, we number the positions clockwise as P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8, all facing the center.
Mahima and Rama:
- Mahima is diagonally opposite Rama.
- This means if Mahima is at position P1, Rama will be at P5 (or any such diagonal pair).
Rama and Sushma:
- Rama is on Sushma's right.
- Since everyone is facing the center, right means clockwise.
- If Rama is at P5, then Sushma must be at P4.
Ravi next to Sushma and opposite Girdhar:
- Ravi is next to Sushma → He can be at P3 or P5.
- Ravi is opposite Girdhar → If Ravi is at P3, then Girdhar must be at P7.
Girdhar is on Chandra's left:
- If Girdhar is at P7, then Chandra must be at P8 (since left means counterclockwise).
Savitri and Shalini:
- Savitri is not on Mahima's right.
- Savitri is opposite Shalini.
- If Mahima is at P1, Mahima's right (clockwise) is P2.
- So, Savitri cannot be at P2.
- The only remaining valid position for Savitri is P6, meaning Shalini is opposite at P2.
Who is on Shalini's right?
- Shalini is at P2, so Shalini's right (clockwise) is P3.
- Ravi is at P3.
