questions.solutionText: Solution Find the pattern in each group by checking positional gaps between consecutive letters: (1) NMQP: N(14) M(13) Q(17) P(16) → gaps: − 1 , + 4 , − 1 → pattern: − 1 , + 4 , − 1 (2) TSWV: T(20) S(19) W(23) V(22) → gaps: − 1 , + 4 , − 1 → same pattern (3) ZYCB: Z(26) Y(25) C(3) B(2) → gaps: − 1 , + 4 , − 1 → same pattern (4) KGIH: K(11) G(7) I(9) H(8) → gaps: − 4 , + 2 , − 1 → different pattern Answer: (4) KGIH
questions.solution: Solution Find the pattern in each group by checking positional gaps between consecutive letters: (1) NMQP: N(14) M(13) Q(17) P(16) → gaps: − 1 , + 4 , − 1 → pattern: − 1 , + 4 , − 1 (2) TSWV: T(20) S(19) W(23) V(22) → gaps: − 1 , + 4 , − 1 → same pattern (3) ZYCB: Z(26) Y(25) C(3) B(2) → gaps: − 1 , + 4 , − 1 → same pattern (4) KGIH: K(11) G(7) I(9) H(8) → gaps: − 4 , + 2 , − 1 → different pattern Answer: (4) KGIH
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Find the pattern in each group by checking positional gaps between consecutive letters:

(1) NMQP: N(14) M(13) Q(17) P(16) → gaps: −1,+4,−1 → pattern: −1,+4,−1

(2) TSWV: T(20) S(19) W(23) V(22) → gaps: −1,+4,−1 → same pattern

(3) ZYCB: Z(26) Y(25) C(3) B(2) → gaps: −1,+4,−1 → same pattern

(4) KGIH: K(11) G(7) I(9) H(8) → gaps: −4,+2,−1 → different pattern

Answer: (4) KGIH

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Mrs. Susheela celebrated her wedding anniversary on Tuesday, 30 th September 1997. When will she celebrate her next wedding anniversary on the same day?
questions.questionText: Mrs. Susheela celebrated her wedding anniversary on Tuesday, 30 th September 1997. When will she celebrate her next wedding anniversary on the same day?
questions.question: Mrs. Susheela celebrated her wedding anniversary on Tuesday, 30 th September 1997. When will she celebrate her next wedding anniversary on the same day?
questions.prompt: Mrs. Susheela celebrated her wedding anniversary on Tuesday, 30 th September 1997. When will she celebrate her next wedding anniversary on the same day?
questions.html: Mrs. Susheela celebrated her wedding anniversary on Tuesday, 30th September 1997. When will she celebrate her next wedding anniversary on the same day?
questions.questionHtml: Mrs. Susheela celebrated her wedding anniversary on Tuesday, 30th September 1997. When will she celebrate her next wedding anniversary on the same day?
questions.solutionText: Solution Count odd days from 30 Sep 1997 to 30 Sep each subsequent year until odd days = 0 (same day, Tuesday). Odd days per year: normal year = 1 , leap year = 2 1998: normal → 1 1999: normal → 1 2000: leap → 2 2001: normal → 1 2002: normal → 1 2003: normal → 1 Total odd days after 1997 up to 30 Sep 2003 = 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7 ≡ 0 ( mod 7 ) 0 odd days ⇒ same day (Tuesday) Answer: (1) 30 September 2003
questions.solution: Solution Count odd days from 30 Sep 1997 to 30 Sep each subsequent year until odd days = 0 (same day, Tuesday). Odd days per year: normal year = 1 , leap year = 2 1998: normal → 1 1999: normal → 1 2000: leap → 2 2001: normal → 1 2002: normal → 1 2003: normal → 1 Total odd days after 1997 up to 30 Sep 2003 = 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7 ≡ 0 ( mod 7 ) 0 odd days ⇒ same day (Tuesday) Answer: (1) 30 September 2003
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Count odd days from 30 Sep 1997 to 30 Sep each subsequent year until odd days =0 (same day, Tuesday).

Odd days per year: normal year =1, leap year =2

1998: normal → 1
1999: normal → 1
2000: leap → 2
2001: normal → 1
2002: normal → 1
2003: normal → 1

Total odd days after 1997 up to 30 Sep 2003 =1+1+2+1+1+1=7≡0(mod7)

0 odd days ⇒ same day (Tuesday)

Answer: (1) 30 September 2003

questions.solutionText: Solution According to question The mother of the mother of the woman is the maternal grandmother of the woman and the only son of the maternal grandmother is the maternal uncle of the woman. Hence the man is the maternal uncle of the woman.

questions.text: The following line graph shows the percentage growth rate of the "Sustainable Energy" sector and the "Traditional Power" sector in a specific country over four years. Based on the graph, which of the following logical inferences is definitely true?

questions.question: The following line graph shows the percentage growth rate of the "Sustainable Energy" sector and the "Traditional Power" sector in a specific country over four years. Based on the graph, which of the following logical inferences is definitely true?
questions.prompt: The following line graph shows the percentage growth rate of the "Sustainable Energy" sector and the "Traditional Power" sector in a specific country over four years. Based on the graph, which of the following logical inferences is definitely true?
questions.html:
The following line graph shows the percentage growth rate of the "Sustainable Energy" sector and the "Traditional Power" sector in a specific country over four years.

Based on the graph, which of the following logical inferences is definitely true?
questions.questionHtml:
The following line graph shows the percentage growth rate of the "Sustainable Energy" sector and the "Traditional Power" sector in a specific country over four years.

Based on the graph, which of the following logical inferences is definitely true?
questions.solutionText: Solution The graph shows percentage growth rates, not absolute values. This is a critical distinction. (1) Sustainable Energy grew at 12% in Year 2 and 18% in Year 3 and 25% in Year 4. Since growth rate is positive every year, revenue increases each year. So Year 4 revenue > Year 2 revenue. This is definitely true. (2) Traditional Power had − 8 % growth in Year 3, meaning its revenue fell from Year 2 to Year 3. So, this inference is not true. (3) Growth rate ≠ total production or revenue. We have no information about base values in Year 1, so this cannot be inferred. (4) "Total market value gap" cannot be determined from growth rates alone without knowing absolute base values. Answer: (1) is definitely true
questions.solution: Solution The graph shows percentage growth rates, not absolute values. This is a critical distinction. (1) Sustainable Energy grew at 12% in Year 2 and 18% in Year 3 and 25% in Year 4. Since growth rate is positive every year, revenue increases each year. So Year 4 revenue > Year 2 revenue. This is definitely true. (2) Traditional Power had − 8 % growth in Year 3, meaning its revenue fell from Year 2 to Year 3. So, this inference is not true. (3) Growth rate ≠ total production or revenue. We have no information about base values in Year 1, so this cannot be inferred. (4) "Total market value gap" cannot be determined from growth rates alone without knowing absolute base values. Answer: (1) is definitely true
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The graph shows percentage growth rates, not absolute values. This is a critical distinction.

(1) Sustainable Energy grew at 12% in Year 2 and 18% in Year 3 and 25% in Year 4. Since growth rate is positive every year, revenue increases each year. So Year 4 revenue > Year 2 revenue. This is definitely true.

(2) Traditional Power had −8% growth in Year 3, meaning its revenue fell from Year 2 to Year 3. So, this inference is not true.

(3) Growth rate ≠ total production or revenue. We have no information about base values in Year 1, so this cannot be inferred.

(4) "Total market value gap" cannot be determined from growth rates alone without knowing absolute base values.

Answer: (1) is definitely true

questions.questionHtml:
In the question below are given two statements followed by two conclusions numbered I and II. You have to take the two given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance from commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows from the two given statements.

Statements:
1. All researchers are hardworking people.
2. Some hardworking people are not well-rested.

Conclusions:
- I. Some researchers are not well-rested.
- II. No researcher is well-rested.
questions.solutionText: Solution Statement 1: All researchers are hardworking people. Statement 2: Some hardworking people are not well-rested. Conclusion I: Some researchers are not well-rested. All researchers are hardworking. Some hardworking people are not well-rested. The "some" in statement 2 may or may not overlap with researchers — it is possible but not definite. So Conclusion I does not necessarily follow. Conclusion II: No researcher is well-rested. This is too strong a claim. Statement 2 only says "some" hardworking people are not well-rested, not all. So Conclusion II does not follow. Answer: D) Neither I nor II follows

So Conclusion I does not necessarily follow. Conclusion II: No researcher is well-rested. This is too strong a claim. Statement 2 only says "some" hardworking people are not well-rested, not all. So Conclusion II does not follow. Answer: D) Neither I nor II follows
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Statement 1: All researchers are hardworking people.
Statement 2: Some hardworking people are not well-rested.

Conclusion I: Some researchers are not well-rested.
All researchers are hardworking. Some hardworking people are not well-rested. The "some" in statement 2 may or may not overlap with researchers — it is possible but not definite. So Conclusion I does not necessarily follow.

Conclusion II: No researcher is well-rested.
This is too strong a claim. Statement 2 only says "some" hardworking people are not well-rested, not all. So Conclusion II does not follow.

Answer: D) Neither I nor II follows

The sets of numbers given in the alternatives are represented by two classes of alphabets as in the matrix given below. The columns and rows of Matrix are numbered from 0 to 6 . A letter from the matrix can be represented first by its row and next by its column, e.g. 'A' can be represented by 42 , 62 , etc. and 'P' can be represented by 15 , 43 , etc. Similarly, you have to identify the set for the word ' CALM'. questions.solutionText: Solution Logic: Look for the number that represents its respective word in the given Matrix, first take the number from the column and then from the row. So, C → 44 A → 62 L → 65 M → 51 Therefore, the number set 44 , 62 , 65 , 51 represents 'CALM'.
questions.solution: Solution Logic: Look for the number that represents its respective word in the given Matrix, first take the number from the column and then from the row. So, C → 44 A → 62 L → 65 M → 51 Therefore, the number set 44 , 62 , 65 , 51 represents 'CALM'.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Logic: Look for the number that represents its respective word in the given Matrix, first take the number from the column and then from the row.

So,
C →44
A →62
L →65
M →51

Therefore, the number set 44, 62, 65, 51 represents 'CALM'.

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Interest on Loans over the years:
1998: 23.4, 1999: 32.5, 2000: 41.6, 2001: 36.4, 2002: 49.4

Total =23.4+32.5+41.6+36.4+49.4=183.3

Average =183.35=36.66 lakhs

Answer: (4) Rs. 36.66 lakhs

questions.questionHtml:
In each question below are given some statements followed by some conclusions. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows/follow from the given statements, disregarding commonly known facts.

Statements:
Some poles are pots.
All pots are plants.
Some plants are leaves.

Conclusions:
Some poles are plants.
All plants being leaves is a possibility
questions.solutionText: Solution Statements: Some poles are pots. (I) All pots are plants. (II) Some plants are leaves. (III) Conclusion I: Some poles are plants. From (I): Some poles are pots. From (II): All pots are plants. ⇒ Some poles are plants. Follows directly. Conclusion II: All plants being leaves is a possibility. From (III): Some plants are leaves. The remaining plants are not stated to be anything else, so it is possible (not contradicted) that all plants are leaves. ⇒ This is a possibility. Follows. Answer: (3) Both conclusions I and II follow
questions.solution: Solution Statements: Some poles are pots. (I) All pots are plants. (II) Some plants are leaves. (III) Conclusion I: Some poles are plants. From (I): Some poles are pots. From (II): All pots are plants. ⇒ Some poles are plants. Follows directly. Conclusion II: All plants being leaves is a possibility. From (III): Some plants are leaves. The remaining plants are not stated to be anything else, so it is possible (not contradicted) that all plants are leaves. ⇒ This is a possibility. Follows. Answer: (3) Both conclusions I and II follow
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Statements:
Some poles are pots. (I)
All pots are plants. (II)
Some plants are leaves. (III)

Conclusion I: Some poles are plants.
From (I): Some poles are pots. From (II): All pots are plants.
⇒ Some poles are plants. Follows directly.

Conclusion II: All plants being leaves is a possibility.
From (III): Some plants are leaves. The remaining plants are not stated to be anything else, so it is possible (not contradicted) that all plants are leaves.
⇒ This is a possibility. Follows.

Answer: (3) Both conclusions I and II follow

questions.text: Which of the following conclusions is logically valid based on the statement given below? Statement: Most of the Indian states existed before independence. Conclusions: (I) Some Indian States existed before independence. (II) All Indian States did not exist before independence.

questions.html:
Which of the following conclusions is logically valid based on the statement given below?

Statement: Most of the Indian states existed before independence.

questions.questionHtml:
Which of the following conclusions is logically valid based on the statement given below?

Statement: Most of the Indian states existed before independence.

Conclusions:

(I) Some Indian States existed before independence.

(II) All Indian States did not exist before independence.
questions.solutionText: Solution Statement: Most of the Indian states existed before independence. Conclusion I: Some Indian states existed before independence. "Most" implies more than half ⇒ "some" (at least one) is definitely true. Conclusion I follows. Conclusion II: All Indian states did not exist before independence. "Most" does not mean "all did not exist." In fact, most existing before independence contradicts the idea that all did not exist. Conclusion II does not follow. Answer: (1) Only (I) is implied
questions.solution: Solution Statement: Most of the Indian states existed before independence. Conclusion I: Some Indian states existed before independence. "Most" implies more than half ⇒ "some" (at least one) is definitely true. Conclusion I follows. Conclusion II: All Indian states did not exist before independence. "Most" does not mean "all did not exist." In fact, most existing before independence contradicts the idea that all did not exist. Conclusion II does not follow. Answer: (1) Only (I) is implied
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Statement: Most of the Indian states existed before independence.

Conclusion I: Some Indian states existed before independence.
"Most" implies more than half ⇒ "some" (at least one) is definitely true. Conclusion I follows.

Conclusion II: All Indian states did not exist before independence.
"Most" does not mean "all did not exist." In fact, most existing before independence contradicts the idea that all did not exist. Conclusion II does not follow.

Answer: (1) Only (I) is implied

In the question below are given two statements followed by two conclusions numbered I and II. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance from commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows from the given statements.

Statements:
1. All students who score above 95% in the qualifying exam are eligible for the National Merit Scholarship.
2. Rohit was not awarded the National Merit Scholarship.

Conclusions:
- I. Rohit scored less than 95% in the qualifying exam.
- II. Scoring above 95% is the only criteria to receive the National Merit Scholarship.
questions.questionHtml:
In the question below are given two statements followed by two conclusions numbered I and II. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance from commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows from the given statements.

Statements:
1. All students who score above 95% in the qualifying exam are eligible for the National Merit Scholarship.
2. Rohit was not awarded the National Merit Scholarship.

Conclusions:
- I. Rohit scored less than 95% in the qualifying exam.
- II. Scoring above 95% is the only criteria to receive the National Merit Scholarship.
questions.solutionText: Solution Statement 1: Score > 95 % ⇒ eligible for scholarship (All students scoring above 95% are eligible) Statement 2: Rohit was NOT awarded the scholarship. Conclusion I: Rohit scored less than 95 % The statement only says scoring above 95% guarantees eligibility, but does NOT say it is the only way to be denied. Rohit may have scored above 95% but still not received it for other reasons (e.g. other criteria). So Conclusion I does NOT necessarily follow. Conclusion II: Scoring above 95% is the only criterion. The statement never says this is the only criterion. This goes beyond what is stated. So Conclusion II does NOT follow. Answer: C) Neither I nor II follows
questions.solution: Solution Statement 1: Score > 95 % ⇒ eligible for scholarship (All students scoring above 95% are eligible) Statement 2: Rohit was NOT awarded the scholarship. Conclusion I: Rohit scored less than 95 % The statement only says scoring above 95% guarantees eligibility, but does NOT say it is the only way to be denied. Rohit may have scored above 95% but still not received it for other reasons (e.g. other criteria). So Conclusion I does NOT necessarily follow. Conclusion II: Scoring above 95% is the only criterion. The statement never says this is the only criterion. This goes beyond what is stated. So Conclusion II does NOT follow. Answer: C) Neither I nor II follows
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Statement 1: Score >95% ⇒ eligible for scholarship (All students scoring above 95% are eligible)
Statement 2: Rohit was NOT awarded the scholarship.

Conclusion I: Rohit scored less than 95%
The statement only says scoring above 95% guarantees eligibility, but does NOT say it is the only way to be denied. Rohit may have scored above 95% but still not received it for other reasons (e.g. other criteria). So Conclusion I does NOT necessarily follow.

Conclusion II: Scoring above 95% is the only criterion.
The statement never says this is the only criterion. This goes beyond what is stated. So Conclusion II does NOT follow.

Answer: C) Neither I nor II follows

questions.text: Study the data provided below regarding the revenue streams of a startup and answer the question that follows. A digital media company derives its revenue from three sources: Subscriptions, Ad-Revenue, and Merchandise. In Quarter 1 (Q1), the total revenue was Rs.200 Crores. The total revenue increased by 10 % in Q2. In Q3, the total revenue decreased by 10 % (relative to Q2). In Q4, the total revenue increased by 20 % (relative to Q3). The following table shows the percentage distribution of these revenue streams: Quarter Subscriptions (%) Ad-Revenue (%) Merchandise (%) Q1 40% 30% 30% Q2 35% 40% 25% Q3 30% 50% 20% Q4 45% 30% 25% Question: Which of the following statements is/are definitely true? 1. The absolute value of Ad-Revenue in Q3 was higher than in Q2. 2. The amount earned from Merchandise in Q4 was less than the amount earned from Merchandise in Q1. 3. Subscription revenue showed a consistent quarter-on-quarter increase in absolute terms.

questions.html:
Study the data provided below regarding the revenue streams of a startup and answer the question that follows.

A digital media company derives its revenue from three sources: Subscriptions, Ad-Revenue, and Merchandise.

In Quarter 1 (Q1), the total revenue was Rs.200 Crores.
The total revenue increased by 10% in Q2.
In Q3, the total revenue decreased by 10% (relative to Q2).
In Q4, the total revenue increased by 20% (relative to Q3).

The following table shows the percentage distribution of these revenue streams:

QuarterSubscriptions (%)Ad-Revenue (%)Merchandise (%)
Q140%30%30%
Q235%40%25%
Q330%50%20%
Q445%30%25%

Question: Which of the following statements is/are definitely true?

1. The absolute value of Ad-Revenue in Q3 was higher than in Q2.
2. The amount earned from Merchandise in Q4 was less than the amount earned from Merchandise in Q1.
3. Subscription revenue showed a consistent quarter-on-quarter increase in absolute terms.
questions.questionHtml:
Study the data provided below regarding the revenue streams of a startup and answer the question that follows.

A digital media company derives its revenue from three sources: Subscriptions, Ad-Revenue, and Merchandise.

In Quarter 1 (Q1), the total revenue was Rs.200 Crores.
The total revenue increased by 10% in Q2.
In Q3, the total revenue decreased by 10% (relative to Q2).
In Q4, the total revenue increased by 20% (relative to Q3).

The following table shows the percentage distribution of these revenue streams:

QuarterSubscriptions (%)Ad-Revenue (%)Merchandise (%)
Q140%30%30%
Q235%40%25%
Q330%50%20%
Q445%30%25%

Question: Which of the following statements is/are definitely true?

1. The absolute value of Ad-Revenue in Q3 was higher than in Q2.
2. The amount earned from Merchandise in Q4 was less than the amount earned from Merchandise in Q1.
3. Subscription revenue showed a consistent quarter-on-quarter increase in absolute terms.
questions.solutionText: Solution Total revenues: Q1 = 200, Q2 = 200 × 1.1 = 220, Q3 = 220 × 0.9 = 198, Q4 = 198 × 1.2 = 237.6 (all in Crores) Statement 1: Ad-Revenue Q2 = 40 % of 220 = 88, Q3 = 50 % of 198 = 99 99 > 88 ⇒ True Statement 2: Merchandise Q1 = 30 % of 200 = 60, Q4 = 25 % of 237.6 = 59.4 59.4 < 60 ⇒ True Statement 3: Subscriptions Q1 = 40 % of 200 = 80, Q2 = 35 % of 220 = 77, Q3 = 30 % of 198 = 59.4, Q4 = 45 % of 237.6 = 106.92 Q2 < Q1 ⇒ not a consistent increase ⇒ False Answer: C) 1 and 2 only
questions.solution: Solution Total revenues: Q1 = 200, Q2 = 200 × 1.1 = 220, Q3 = 220 × 0.9 = 198, Q4 = 198 × 1.2 = 237.6 (all in Crores) Statement 1: Ad-Revenue Q2 = 40 % of 220 = 88, Q3 = 50 % of 198 = 99 99 > 88 ⇒ True Statement 2: Merchandise Q1 = 30 % of 200 = 60, Q4 = 25 % of 237.6 = 59.4 59.4 < 60 ⇒ True Statement 3: Subscriptions Q1 = 40 % of 200 = 80, Q2 = 35 % of 220 = 77, Q3 = 30 % of 198 = 59.4, Q4 = 45 % of 237.6 = 106.92 Q2 < Q1 ⇒ not a consistent increase ⇒ False Answer: C) 1 and 2 only
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Total revenues:
Q1 = 200, Q2 = 200 × 1.1 = 220, Q3 = 220 × 0.9 = 198, Q4 = 198 × 1.2 = 237.6 (all in Crores)

Statement 1: Ad-Revenue
Q2 = 40% of 220 = 88, Q3 = 50% of 198 = 99
99 > 88 ⇒ True

Statement 2: Merchandise
Q1 = 30% of 200 = 60, Q4 = 25% of 237.6 = 59.4
59.4 < 60 ⇒ True

Statement 3: Subscriptions
Q1 = 40% of 200 = 80, Q2 = 35% of 220 = 77, Q3 = 30% of 198 = 59.4, Q4 = 45% of 237.6 = 106.92
Q2 < Q1 ⇒ not a consistent increase ⇒ False

Answer: C) 1 and 2 only

questions.prompt: Let S = 1,2 , 3 , … . 9 . For k = 1,2 , … 5 , let N k be the number of subsets of S , each containing five elements out of which exactly k are odd. Then N 1 + N 2 + N 3 + N 4 + N 5 =
questions.html: Let S=1,2,3,….9. For k=1,2,…5, let Nk be the number of subsets of S, each containing five elements out of which exactly k are odd. Then N1+N2+N3+N4+N5=
questions.questionHtml: Let S=1,2,3,….9. For k=1,2,…5, let Nk be the number of subsets of S, each containing five elements out of which exactly k are odd. Then N1+N2+N3+N4+N5=
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
S={1,2,3,...,9} has 5 odd numbers {1,3,5,7,9} and 4 even numbers {2,4,6,8}.

Nk = number of 5-element subsets with exactly k odd numbers.

Nk=(5k)(45−k)

N1=(51)(44)=5×1=5
N2=(52)(43)=10×4=40
N3=(53)(42)=10×6=60
N4=(54)(41)=5×4=20
N5=(55)(40)=1×1=1

N1+N2+N3+N4+N5=5+40+60+20+1=126

Answer: (4) 126

questions.solutionText: Solution Given that: Equation of Hyperbola x 2 a 2 - y 2 b 2 = 1 ∵ P Q Is the double ordinate. Let M P = M Q = l . Given that ∆ O P Q is an equilateral, then O P = O Q = P Q ⇒ O P 2 = O Q 2 = P Q 2 ⇒ a 2 b 2 b 2 + l 2 + l 2 = a 2 b 2 b 2 + l 2 + l 2 = 4 l 2 ⇒ a 2 b 2 b 2 + l 2 = 3 l 2 ⇒ a 2 = l 2 3 - a 2 b 2 ⇒ l 2 = a 2 b 2 3 b 2 - a 2 > 0 ∴ 3 b 2 - a 2 > 0 ⇒ 3 b 2 > a 2 ⇒ 3 a 2 e 2 - 1 > a 2 ∴ e > 2 3

⇒ 3b2>a2

∴ e>23

questions.text: lim h → 0 f 2 h + 2 + h 2 - f 2 f h - h 2 + 1 - f 1 , given that f ′ 2 = 6 and f ′ 1 = 4
questions.questionText: lim h → 0 f 2 h + 2 + h 2 - f 2 f h - h 2 + 1 - f 1 , given that f ′ 2 = 6 and f ′ 1 = 4
questions.question: lim h → 0 f 2 h + 2 + h 2 - f 2 f h - h 2 + 1 - f 1 , given that f ′ 2 = 6 and f ′ 1 = 4
questions.prompt: lim h → 0 f 2 h + 2 + h 2 - f 2 f h - h 2 + 1 - f 1 , given that f ′ 2 = 6 and f ′ 1 = 4
questions.html: limh→0f2h+2+h2-f2fh-h2+1-f1, given that f′2=6 and f′1=4
questions.questionHtml: limh→0f2h+2+h2-f2fh-h2+1-f1, given that f′2=6 and f′1=4
questions.solutionText: Solution lim h → 0 f ( 2 h + 2 + h 2 ) − f ( 2 ) f ( h − h 2 + 1 ) − f ( 1 ) ( 0 0 form ) Applying L'Hopital's rule, differentiate numerator and denominator with respect to h : Numerator: d d h [ f ( 2 h + 2 + h 2 ) ] = f ′ ( 2 h + 2 + h 2 ) ⋅ ( 2 + 2 h ) Denominator: d d h [ f ( h − h 2 + 1 ) ] = f ′ ( h − h 2 + 1 ) ⋅ ( 1 − 2 h ) lim h → 0 f ′ ( 2 h + 2 + h 2 ) ⋅ ( 2 + 2 h ) f ′ ( h − h 2 + 1 ) ⋅ ( 1 − 2 h ) Substituting h = 0 : = f ′ ( 2 ) ⋅ 2 f ′ ( 1 ) ⋅ 1 = 6 × 2 4 × 1 = 12 4 = 3 Answer: (4) 3
questions.solution: Solution lim h → 0 f ( 2 h + 2 + h 2 ) − f ( 2 ) f ( h − h 2 + 1 ) − f ( 1 ) ( 0 0 form ) Applying L'Hopital's rule, differentiate numerator and denominator with respect to h : Numerator: d d h [ f ( 2 h + 2 + h 2 ) ] = f ′ ( 2 h + 2 + h 2 ) ⋅ ( 2 + 2 h ) Denominator: d d h [ f ( h − h 2 + 1 ) ] = f ′ ( h − h 2 + 1 ) ⋅ ( 1 − 2 h ) lim h → 0 f ′ ( 2 h + 2 + h 2 ) ⋅ ( 2 + 2 h ) f ′ ( h − h 2 + 1 ) ⋅ ( 1 − 2 h ) Substituting h = 0 : = f ′ ( 2 ) ⋅ 2 f ′ ( 1 ) ⋅ 1 = 6 × 2 4 × 1 = 12 4 = 3 Answer: (4) 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
limh→0f(2h+2+h2)−f(2)f(h−h2+1)−f(1) (00 form)

Applying L'Hopital's rule, differentiate numerator and denominator with respect to h:

Numerator: ddh[f(2h+2+h2)]=f′(2h+2+h2)⋅(2+2h)

Denominator: ddh[f(h−h2+1)]=f′(h−h2+1)⋅(1−2h)

limh→0f′(2h+2+h2)⋅(2+2h)f′(h−h2+1)⋅(1−2h)

Substituting h=0:

=f′(2)⋅2f′(1)⋅1=6×24×1=124=3

Answer: (4) 3

questions.solutionText: Solution Let, z = x + i y ∴ z 2 - 2 z = x + i y 2 - 2 x + i y = x 2 + i 2 y 2 + 2 x y i - 2 x - 2 y i = x 2 - y 2 + 2 x y i - 2 x - 2 y i = x 2 - y 2 - 2 x + i 2 x y - 2 y ⇒ R e z 2 - 2 z = x 2 - y 2 - 2 x = 15 ⇒ x 2 - y 2 - 2 x = 15 ⇒ x 2 - 2 x + 1 - y 2 = 15 + 1 ⇒ x - 1 2 - y 2 = 16 ⇒ x - 1 2 - y - 0 2 = 4 2 (which is a rectangular hyperbola).
questions.solution: Solution Let, z = x + i y ∴ z 2 - 2 z = x + i y 2 - 2 x + i y = x 2 + i 2 y 2 + 2 x y i - 2 x - 2 y i = x 2 - y 2 + 2 x y i - 2 x - 2 y i = x 2 - y 2 - 2 x + i 2 x y - 2 y ⇒ R e z 2 - 2 z = x 2 - y 2 - 2 x = 15 ⇒ x 2 - y 2 - 2 x = 15 ⇒ x 2 - 2 x + 1 - y 2 = 15 + 1 ⇒ x - 1 2 - y 2 = 16 ⇒ x - 1 2 - y - 0 2 = 4 2 (which is a rectangular hyperbola).
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let, z=x+iy
∴z2-2z
=x+iy2-2x+iy
=x2+i2y2+2xyi-2x-2yi
=x2-y2+2xyi-2x-2yi
=x2-y2-2x+i2xy-2y
⇒Rez2-2z=x2-y2-2x=15
⇒x2-y2-2x=15
⇒x2-2x+1-y2=15+1
⇒x-12-y2=16
⇒x-12-y-02=42 (which is a rectangular hyperbola).

questions.text: 10 distinct people are to be seated around a circular table. Find the number of ways they can be seated such that: - Two specific people (say A and B) do not sit adjacent, and - Three specific people (say C, D, and E) must sit together (in any order).
questions.questionText: 10 distinct people are to be seated around a circular table. Find the number of ways they can be seated such that: - Two specific people (say A and B) do not sit adjacent, and - Three specific people (say C, D, and E) must sit together (in any order).
questions.question: 10 distinct people are to be seated around a circular table. Find the number of ways they can be seated such that: - Two specific people (say A and B) do not sit adjacent, and - Three specific people (say C, D, and E) must sit together (in any order).
questions.prompt: 10 distinct people are to be seated around a circular table. Find the number of ways they can be seated such that: - Two specific people (say A and B) do not sit adjacent, and - Three specific people (say C, D, and E) must sit together (in any order).
questions.html:
10 distinct people are to be seated around a circular table. Find the number of ways they can be seated such that:
- Two specific people (say A and B) do not sit adjacent, and
- Three specific people (say C, D, and E) must sit together (in any order).
questions.questionHtml:
10 distinct people are to be seated around a circular table. Find the number of ways they can be seated such that:
- Two specific people (say A and B) do not sit adjacent, and
- Three specific people (say C, D, and E) must sit together (in any order).
questions.solutionText: Solution We have 10 people. - C, D, E must sit together → treat as 1 block → reduces to 8 people. - Total circular arrangements = ( 8 − 1 ) ! = 7 ! - CDE can be arranged within block = 3 ! = 6 → Total = 7 ! × 6 Now subtract cases where A and B are adjacent: - Treat A-B as a block → total blocks now = 7 - Circular arrangements = ( 7 − 1 ) ! = 6 ! - A-B can be arranged in 2 ways, CDE in 6 ways → Unwanted = 6 ! × 2 × 6 Final Answer: 7 ! × 6 − 6 ! × 2 × 6
questions.solution: Solution We have 10 people. - C, D, E must sit together → treat as 1 block → reduces to 8 people. - Total circular arrangements = ( 8 − 1 ) ! = 7 ! - CDE can be arranged within block = 3 ! = 6 → Total = 7 ! × 6 Now subtract cases where A and B are adjacent: - Treat A-B as a block → total blocks now = 7 - Circular arrangements = ( 7 − 1 ) ! = 6 ! - A-B can be arranged in 2 ways, CDE in 6 ways → Unwanted = 6 ! × 2 × 6 Final Answer: 7 ! × 6 − 6 ! × 2 × 6
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
We have 10 people.
- C, D, E must sit together → treat as 1 block → reduces to 8 people.
- Total circular arrangements =(8−1)!=7 !
- CDE can be arranged within block =3!=6
→ Total =7!×6
Now subtract cases where A and B are adjacent:
- Treat A-B as a block → total blocks now =7
- Circular arrangements =(7−1)!=6 !
- A-B can be arranged in 2 ways, CDE in 6 ways
→ Unwanted =6!×2×6
Final Answer:
7!×6−6!×2×6

Put y x = t ⇒ y = t x ⇒ d y d x = t + x d t d x . . . . i i From equations (i) and (ii), we get x d t d x = t ( log e t ) ∫ 1 t log e t d t = ∫ 1 x d x ln ( ln t ) = ln x + ln C ln ( ln t ) = ln ( c x ) ln t = c x ln y x = c x .

questions.solutionText: Solution Let, I = ∫ x 2 - 1 x 4 + 3 x 2 + 1 tan - 1 x + 1 x d x On dividing numerator and denominator by x 2 , I = ∫ 1 - 1 x 2 x 2 + 1 x 2 + 3 tan - 1 x + 1 x d x Put x + 1 x = t ⇒ 1 - 1 x 2 d x = d t and x 2 + 1 x 2 = t 2 - 2 ∴ I = ∫ d t t 2 - 2 + 3 tan - 1 t = ∫ d t 1 + t 2 tan - 1 t = log tan - 1 t + C = log tan - 1 x + 1 x + C
questions.solution: Solution Let, I = ∫ x 2 - 1 x 4 + 3 x 2 + 1 tan - 1 x + 1 x d x On dividing numerator and denominator by x 2 , I = ∫ 1 - 1 x 2 x 2 + 1 x 2 + 3 tan - 1 x + 1 x d x Put x + 1 x = t ⇒ 1 - 1 x 2 d x = d t and x 2 + 1 x 2 = t 2 - 2 ∴ I = ∫ d t t 2 - 2 + 3 tan - 1 t = ∫ d t 1 + t 2 tan - 1 t = log tan - 1 t + C = log tan - 1 x + 1 x + C

questions.text: If I n = ∫ tan n ⁡ x d x for n ≥ 2 , then I n + I n - 2 is equal to
questions.questionText: If I n = ∫ tan n ⁡ x d x for n ≥ 2 , then I n + I n - 2 is equal to
questions.question: If I n = ∫ tan n ⁡ x d x for n ≥ 2 , then I n + I n - 2 is equal to

questions.html: If In=∫tann⁡xdx for n≥2, then In+In-2 is equal to
questions.questionHtml: If In=∫tann⁡xdx for n≥2, then In+In-2 is equal to
questions.solutionText: Solution I n = ∫ tan n ⁡ x d x I n + I n − 2 = ∫ tan n ⁡ x d x + ∫ tan n − 2 ⁡ x d x = ∫ tan n − 2 ⁡ x ( tan 2 ⁡ x + 1 ) d x = ∫ tan n − 2 ⁡ x ⋅ sec 2 ⁡ x d x Let t = tan ⁡ x ⇒ d t = sec 2 ⁡ x d x = ∫ t n − 2 d t = t n − 1 n − 1 + c = tan n − 1 ⁡ x n − 1 + c Answer: (2) tan n − 1 ⁡ x n − 1 + c
questions.solution: Solution I n = ∫ tan n ⁡ x d x I n + I n − 2 = ∫ tan n ⁡ x d x + ∫ tan n − 2 ⁡ x d x = ∫ tan n − 2 ⁡ x ( tan 2 ⁡ x + 1 ) d x = ∫ tan n − 2 ⁡ x ⋅ sec 2 ⁡ x d x Let t = tan ⁡ x ⇒ d t = sec 2 ⁡ x d x = ∫ t n − 2 d t = t n − 1 n − 1 + c = tan n − 1 ⁡ x n − 1 + c Answer: (2) tan n − 1 ⁡ x n − 1 + c
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In=∫tann⁡xdx

In+In−2=∫tann⁡xdx+∫tann−2⁡xdx

=∫tann−2⁡x(tan2⁡x+1)dx=∫tann−2⁡x⋅sec2⁡xdx

Let t=tan⁡x ⇒ dt=sec2⁡xdx

=∫tn−2dt=tn−1n−1+c=tann−1⁡xn−1+c

Answer: (2) tann−1⁡xn−1+c

questions.text: Equation of the plane passing through ( − 2 , 2 , 2 ) and ( 2 , − 2 , − 2 ) and perpendicular to the plane 9 x − 13 y − 3 z = 0 is
questions.questionText: Equation of the plane passing through ( − 2 , 2 , 2 ) and ( 2 , − 2 , − 2 ) and perpendicular to the plane 9 x − 13 y − 3 z = 0 is
questions.question: Equation of the plane passing through ( − 2 , 2 , 2 ) and ( 2 , − 2 , − 2 ) and perpendicular to the plane 9 x − 13 y − 3 z = 0 is
questions.prompt: Equation of the plane passing through ( − 2 , 2 , 2 ) and ( 2 , − 2 , − 2 ) and perpendicular to the plane 9 x − 13 y − 3 z = 0 is
questions.html: Equation of the plane passing through (−2,2,2) and (2,−2,−2) and perpendicular to the plane 9x−13y−3z=0 is
questions.questionHtml: Equation of the plane passing through (−2,2,2) and (2,−2,−2) and perpendicular to the plane 9x−13y−3z=0 is
questions.solutionText: Solution Any plane passing through ( − 2 , 2 , 2 ) is A ( x + 2 ) + B ( y − 2 ) + C ( z − 2 ) = 0 ∵ It passes through ( 2 , − 2 , − 2 ) ⇒ 4 A − 4 B − 4 C = 0 It is parallel to 9 x − 13 y − 3 z = 0 ∴ 9 A − 13 B − 3 C = 0 … (ii) Solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), A 12 − 52 = B − 36 + 12 = C − 52 + 36 ⇒ A − 40 = B − 24 = C − 16 ∴ Required equation of plane is − 40 ( x + 2 ) − 24 ( y − 2 ) − 16 ( z − 2 ) = 0 ⇒ − 40 x − 80 − 24 y + 48 − 16 z + 32 = 0 ⇒ 40 x + 24 y + 16 z = 0 ⇒ 5 x + 3 y + 2 z = 0
questions.solution: Solution Any plane passing through ( − 2 , 2 , 2 ) is A ( x + 2 ) + B ( y − 2 ) + C ( z − 2 ) = 0 ∵ It passes through ( 2 , − 2 , − 2 ) ⇒ 4 A − 4 B − 4 C = 0 It is parallel to 9 x − 13 y − 3 z = 0 ∴ 9 A − 13 B − 3 C = 0 … (ii) Solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), A 12 − 52 = B − 36 + 12 = C − 52 + 36 ⇒ A − 40 = B − 24 = C − 16 ∴ Required equation of plane is − 40 ( x + 2 ) − 24 ( y − 2 ) − 16 ( z − 2 ) = 0 ⇒ − 40 x − 80 − 24 y + 48 − 16 z + 32 = 0 ⇒ 40 x + 24 y + 16 z = 0 ⇒ 5 x + 3 y + 2 z = 0
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Any plane passing through (−2,2,2) is A(x+2)+B(y−2)+C(z−2)=0
∵ It passes through (2,−2,−2)
⇒4A−4B−4C=0
It is parallel to 9x−13y−3z=0
∴9A−13B−3C=0… (ii)
Solving Eqs. (i) and (ii),
A12−52=B−36+12=C−52+36⇒A−40=B−24=C−16
∴ Required equation of plane is −40(x+2)−24(y−2)−16(z−2)=0
⇒−40x−80−24y+48−16z+32=0⇒40x+24y+16z=0⇒5x+3y+2z=0

questions.prompt: Function f x = tan π x – π 1 + x 2 where . is GIF. is
questions.html: Function fx=tanπx–π1+x2 where . is GIF. is
questions.questionHtml: Function fx=tanπx–π1+x2 where . is GIF. is
questions.solutionText: Solution f ( x ) = tan ⁡ π [ x − π ] 1 + [ x ] 2 [ x − π ] is an integer for all x (since [ x − π ] ∈ Z always). tan ⁡ π [ x − π ] = tan ⁡ ( n π ) where n ∈ Z tan ⁡ ( n π ) = 0 for all integers n . So numerator = 0 for all x . Denominator = 1 + [ x ] 2 ≥ 1 > 0 for all x , so it never becomes zero. ∴ f ( x ) = 0 everywhere ⇒ constant function, continuous everywhere. Answer: (4) Every where continuous
questions.solution: Solution f ( x ) = tan ⁡ π [ x − π ] 1 + [ x ] 2 [ x − π ] is an integer for all x (since [ x − π ] ∈ Z always). tan ⁡ π [ x − π ] = tan ⁡ ( n π ) where n ∈ Z tan ⁡ ( n π ) = 0 for all integers n . So numerator = 0 for all x . Denominator = 1 + [ x ] 2 ≥ 1 > 0 for all x , so it never becomes zero. ∴ f ( x ) = 0 everywhere ⇒ constant function, continuous everywhere. Answer: (4) Every where continuous
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
f(x)=tan⁡π[x−π]1+[x]2

[x−π] is an integer for all x (since [x−π]∈Z always).

tan⁡π[x−π]=tan⁡(nπ) where n∈Z
tan⁡(nπ)=0 for all integers n.

So numerator =0 for all x.

Denominator =1+[x]2≥1>0 for all x, so it never becomes zero.

∴f(x)=0 everywhere ⇒ constant function, continuous everywhere.

Answer: (4) Every where continuous

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.text: The length of the latus rectum of the parabola 9 x 2 − 6 x + 36 y + 19 = 0
questions.questionText: The length of the latus rectum of the parabola 9 x 2 − 6 x + 36 y + 19 = 0
questions.question: The length of the latus rectum of the parabola 9 x 2 − 6 x + 36 y + 19 = 0
questions.prompt: The length of the latus rectum of the parabola 9 x 2 − 6 x + 36 y + 19 = 0
questions.html: The length of the latus rectum of the parabola 9x2−6x+36y+19=0
questions.questionHtml: The length of the latus rectum of the parabola 9x2−6x+36y+19=0
questions.solutionText: Solution 9 x 2 − 6 x + 36 y + 19 = 0 Complete the square in x : 9 ( x 2 − 2 3 x ) + 36 y + 19 = 0 9 ( x − 1 3 ) 2 − 9 ⋅ 1 9 + 36 y + 19 = 0 9 ( x − 1 3 ) 2 + 36 y + 18 = 0 9 ( x − 1 3 ) 2 = − 36 y − 18 = − 36 ( y + 1 2 ) ( x − 1 3 ) 2 = − 4 ( y + 1 2 ) This is of the form X 2 = − 4 a Y where 4 a = 4 ⇒ a = 1 Length of latus rectum = 4 a = 4 Answer: (4) 4
questions.solution: Solution 9 x 2 − 6 x + 36 y + 19 = 0 Complete the square in x : 9 ( x 2 − 2 3 x ) + 36 y + 19 = 0 9 ( x − 1 3 ) 2 − 9 ⋅ 1 9 + 36 y + 19 = 0 9 ( x − 1 3 ) 2 + 36 y + 18 = 0 9 ( x − 1 3 ) 2 = − 36 y − 18 = − 36 ( y + 1 2 ) ( x − 1 3 ) 2 = − 4 ( y + 1 2 ) This is of the form X 2 = − 4 a Y where 4 a = 4 ⇒ a = 1 Length of latus rectum = 4 a = 4 Answer: (4) 4
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
9x2−6x+36y+19=0

Complete the square in x:
9(x2−23x)+36y+19=0
9(x−13)2−9⋅19+36y+19=0
9(x−13)2+36y+18=0
9(x−13)2=−36y−18=−36(y+12)

(x−13)2=−4(y+12)

This is of the form X2=−4aY where 4a=4 ⇒ a=1

Length of latus rectum =4a=4

Answer: (4) 4

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.text: Which one of the following is not true always?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not true always?
questions.question: Which one of the following is not true always?
questions.prompt: Which one of the following is not true always?
questions.html: Which one of the following is not true always?
questions.questionHtml: Which one of the following is not true always?
questions.solutionText: Solution (1) If f ( x ) is not continuous at x = a , it cannot be differentiable there. True always. (2) Continuity does not imply differentiability. Classic counterexample: f ( x ) = | x | is continuous at x = 0 but not differentiable at x = 0 . So this is NOT always true. (3) Sum of two differentiable functions is differentiable. True always. (4) If f ( x ) is continuous at x = a , then lim x → a f ( x ) = f ( a ) , which exists. True always. Answer: (2)
questions.solution: Solution (1) If f ( x ) is not continuous at x = a , it cannot be differentiable there. True always. (2) Continuity does not imply differentiability. Classic counterexample: f ( x ) = | x | is continuous at x = 0 but not differentiable at x = 0 . So this is NOT always true. (3) Sum of two differentiable functions is differentiable. True always. (4) If f ( x ) is continuous at x = a , then lim x → a f ( x ) = f ( a ) , which exists. True always. Answer: (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(1) If f(x) is not continuous at x=a, it cannot be differentiable there. True always.

(2) Continuity does not imply differentiability. Classic counterexample: f(x)=|x| is continuous at x=0 but not differentiable at x=0. So this is NOT always true.

(3) Sum of two differentiable functions is differentiable. True always.

(4) If f(x) is continuous at x=a, then limx→af(x)=f(a), which exists. True always.

Answer: (2)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.text: The general solution of the differential equation x 2 ( 1 + y 3 ) d x = y 2 ( 1 + x 3 ) d y is
questions.questionText: The general solution of the differential equation x 2 ( 1 + y 3 ) d x = y 2 ( 1 + x 3 ) d y is
questions.question: The general solution of the differential equation x 2 ( 1 + y 3 ) d x = y 2 ( 1 + x 3 ) d y is
questions.prompt: The general solution of the differential equation x 2 ( 1 + y 3 ) d x = y 2 ( 1 + x 3 ) d y is
questions.html: The general solution of the differential equation x2(1+y3)dx=y2(1+x3)dy is
questions.questionHtml: The general solution of the differential equation x2(1+y3)dx=y2(1+x3)dy is
questions.solutionText: Solution On rearranging the given equation, we reduce the equation in the form. x 2 d x 1 + x 3 = y 2 d y 1 + y 3 Integrating both side, using method of substitution let ( 1 + x 3 ) = t , on differentiating both sides we get 3 x 2 = d t d x ⇒ x 2 d x = d t 3 Now, ∫ x 2 d x 1 + x 3 = 1 3 ∫ d t t = 1 3 ln t Doing similarly for right-hand side we obtain, 1 3 ln ( 1 + x 3 ) = 1 3 ln ( 1 + y 3 ) + ln C 3 ln 1 + x 3 = ln c ( 1 + y 3 ) ( 1 + x 3 ) = ( c ( 1 + y 3 ) )
questions.solution: Solution On rearranging the given equation, we reduce the equation in the form. x 2 d x 1 + x 3 = y 2 d y 1 + y 3 Integrating both side, using method of substitution let ( 1 + x 3 ) = t , on differentiating both sides we get 3 x 2 = d t d x ⇒ x 2 d x = d t 3 Now, ∫ x 2 d x 1 + x 3 = 1 3 ∫ d t t = 1 3 ln t Doing similarly for right-hand side we obtain, 1 3 ln ( 1 + x 3 ) = 1 3 ln ( 1 + y 3 ) + ln C 3 ln 1 + x 3 = ln c ( 1 + y 3 ) ( 1 + x 3 ) = ( c ( 1 + y 3 ) )
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

On rearranging the given equation, we reduce the equation in the form.

x2dx1+x3=y2dy1+y3

Integrating both side, using method of substitution

let (1+x3)=t, on differentiating both sides we get 3x2=dtdx⇒x2dx=dt3

Now,∫x2dx1+x3=13∫dtt=13lnt

Doing similarly for right-hand side we obtain,13ln(1+x3)=13ln(1+y3)+lnC3

ln1+x3=lnc(1+y3)

(1+x3)=(c(1+y3))

questions.solutionText: Solution The equation of line PQ passing through ( 1 , 2 ) is y − 2 = m ( x − 1 ) A ( △ OPQ ) = 1 2 × OP × OQ = 1 2 ( 1 − 2 m ) ( 2 − m ) = 1 2 ( 4 − m − 4 m ) A = 2 − m 2 − 2 m ∴ dA dm = − 1 2 + 2 m 2 Now dA dm = 0 ⇒ − 1 2 + 2 m 2 = 0 ⇒ m 2 = 4 ⇒ m = ± 2 d 2 A dm 2 = − 4 m 3 At m = 2 d 2 A dm 2 < 0 At m = − 2 , d 2 A dm 2 > 0 ∴ Area of △ OPQ will be least at m = − 2 ⇒ Slope of the line P Q = − 2

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The equation of line PQ passing through (1,2) is y−2=m(x−1)

A(△OPQ)=12×OP×OQ=12(1−2 m)(2−m)=12(4−m−4 m)A=2−m2−2 m∴dAdm=−12+2 m2NowdAdm=0⇒−12+2 m2=0⇒m2=4⇒m=±2d2 Adm2=−4 m3Atm=2d2 Adm2<0
At m=−2,d2 Adm2>0
∴ Area of △OPQ will be least at m=−2
⇒ Slope of the line PQ=−2

Solution
dPdx=100-12x

questions.text: If the normal at the point P 6 , 3 on the hyperbola x 2 a 2 - y 2 b 2 = 1 intersects the x -axis at 9 , 0 , then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is:
questions.questionText: If the normal at the point P 6 , 3 on the hyperbola x 2 a 2 - y 2 b 2 = 1 intersects the x -axis at 9 , 0 , then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is:

questions.prompt: If the normal at the point P 6 , 3 on the hyperbola x 2 a 2 - y 2 b 2 = 1 intersects the x -axis at 9 , 0 , then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is:
questions.html: If the normal at the point P6,3 on the hyperbola x2a2-y2b2=1 intersects the x-axis at 9,0, then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is:
questions.questionHtml: If the normal at the point P6,3 on the hyperbola x2a2-y2b2=1 intersects the x-axis at 9,0, then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is:
questions.solutionText: Solution Equation of normal to hyperbola x 2 a 2 − y 2 b 2 = 1 at point ( x 1 , y 1 ) : a 2 x x 1 + b 2 y y 1 = a 2 + b 2 At P ( 6 , 3 ) : a 2 x 6 + b 2 y 3 = a 2 + b 2 Normal passes through ( 9 , 0 ) : 9 a 2 6 + 0 = a 2 + b 2 3 a 2 2 = a 2 + b 2 b 2 = a 2 2 ... (i) Since P ( 6 , 3 ) lies on hyperbola: 36 a 2 − 9 b 2 = 1 ... (ii) Substituting (i) into (ii): 36 a 2 − 18 a 2 = 1 18 a 2 = 1 ⇒ a 2 = 18 , b 2 = 9 e = 1 + b 2 a 2 = 1 + 9 18 = 3 2 Answer: (2) 3 2
questions.solution: Solution Equation of normal to hyperbola x 2 a 2 − y 2 b 2 = 1 at point ( x 1 , y 1 ) : a 2 x x 1 + b 2 y y 1 = a 2 + b 2 At P ( 6 , 3 ) : a 2 x 6 + b 2 y 3 = a 2 + b 2 Normal passes through ( 9 , 0 ) : 9 a 2 6 + 0 = a 2 + b 2 3 a 2 2 = a 2 + b 2 b 2 = a 2 2 ... (i) Since P ( 6 , 3 ) lies on hyperbola: 36 a 2 − 9 b 2 = 1 ... (ii) Substituting (i) into (ii): 36 a 2 − 18 a 2 = 1 18 a 2 = 1 ⇒ a 2 = 18 , b 2 = 9 e = 1 + b 2 a 2 = 1 + 9 18 = 3 2 Answer: (2) 3 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Equation of normal to hyperbola x2a2−y2b2=1 at point (x1,y1):

a2xx1+b2yy1=a2+b2

At P(6,3): a2x6+b2y3=a2+b2

Normal passes through (9,0):
9a26+0=a2+b2
3a22=a2+b2
b2=a22 ... (i)

Since P(6,3) lies on hyperbola:
36a2−9b2=1 ... (ii)

Substituting (i) into (ii):
36a2−18a2=1
18a2=1⇒a2=18, b2=9

e=1+b2a2=1+918=32

Answer: (2) 32

questions.html:
Let Vr denotes the sum of the first r terms of an arithmetic progression (AP) whose first term is r and the common difference is (2r−1). Let Tr=Vr+1−Vr−2 for r=1,2,…
Tr is always
questions.questionHtml:
Let Vr denotes the sum of the first r terms of an arithmetic progression (AP) whose first term is r and the common difference is (2r−1). Let Tr=Vr+1−Vr−2 for r=1,2,…
Tr is always
questions.solutionText: Solution AP with first term = r , common difference = 2 r − 1 V r = r 2 [ 2 r + ( r − 1 ) ( 2 r − 1 ) ] = r 2 [ 2 r + 2 r 2 − 3 r + 1 ] = r 2 [ 2 r 2 − r + 1 ] T r = V r + 1 − V r − 2 V r + 1 = ( r + 1 ) 2 [ 2 ( r + 1 ) 2 − ( r + 1 ) + 1 ] = ( r + 1 ) 2 [ 2 r 2 + 4 r + 2 − r ] = ( r + 1 ) ( 2 r 2 + 3 r + 2 ) 2 V r + 1 − V r = ( r + 1 ) ( 2 r 2 + 3 r + 2 ) − r ( 2 r 2 − r + 1 ) 2 Numerator = 2 r 3 + 3 r 2 + 2 r + 2 r 2 + 3 r + 2 − 2 r 3 + r 2 − r = 6 r 2 + 4 r + 2 V r + 1 − V r = 6 r 2 + 4 r + 2 2 = 3 r 2 + 2 r + 1 T r = 3 r 2 + 2 r + 1 − 2 = 3 r 2 + 2 r − 1 = ( 3 r − 1 ) ( r + 1 ) T r = ( 3 r − 1 ) ( r + 1 ) : product of two integers both > 1 for r ≥ 1 ⇒ always composite. Answer: (4) a composite number
questions.solution: Solution AP with first term = r , common difference = 2 r − 1 V r = r 2 [ 2 r + ( r − 1 ) ( 2 r − 1 ) ] = r 2 [ 2 r + 2 r 2 − 3 r + 1 ] = r 2 [ 2 r 2 − r + 1 ] T r = V r + 1 − V r − 2 V r + 1 = ( r + 1 ) 2 [ 2 ( r + 1 ) 2 − ( r + 1 ) + 1 ] = ( r + 1 ) 2 [ 2 r 2 + 4 r + 2 − r ] = ( r + 1 ) ( 2 r 2 + 3 r + 2 ) 2 V r + 1 − V r = ( r + 1 ) ( 2 r 2 + 3 r + 2 ) − r ( 2 r 2 − r + 1 ) 2 Numerator = 2 r 3 + 3 r 2 + 2 r + 2 r 2 + 3 r + 2 − 2 r 3 + r 2 − r = 6 r 2 + 4 r + 2 V r + 1 − V r = 6 r 2 + 4 r + 2 2 = 3 r 2 + 2 r + 1 T r = 3 r 2 + 2 r + 1 − 2 = 3 r 2 + 2 r − 1 = ( 3 r − 1 ) ( r + 1 ) T r = ( 3 r − 1 ) ( r + 1 ) : product of two integers both > 1 for r ≥ 1 ⇒ always composite. Answer: (4) a composite number
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
AP with first term =r, common difference =2r−1

Vr=r2[2r+(r−1)(2r−1)]=r2[2r+2r2−3r+1]=r2[2r2−r+1]

Tr=Vr+1−Vr−2

Vr+1=(r+1)2[2(r+1)2−(r+1)+1]=(r+1)2[2r2+4r+2−r]=(r+1)(2r2+3r+2)2

Vr+1−Vr=(r+1)(2r2+3r+2)−r(2r2−r+1)2

Numerator =2r3+3r2+2r+2r2+3r+2−2r3+r2−r=6r2+4r+2

Vr+1−Vr=6r2+4r+22=3r2+2r+1

Tr=3r2+2r+1−2=3r2+2r−1=(3r−1)(r+1)

Tr=(3r−1)(r+1): product of two integers both >1 for r≥1 ⇒ always composite.

Answer: (4) a composite number

questions.solutionText: Solution I = ∫ 1 e x 2 ln ⁡ x d x Integration by parts: u = ln ⁡ x , d v = x 2 d x d u = 1 x d x , v = x 3 3 I = [ x 3 3 ln ⁡ x ] 1 e − ∫ 1 e x 3 3 ⋅ 1 x d x = [ x 3 3 ln ⁡ x ] 1 e − 1 3 ∫ 1 e x 2 d x = ( e 3 3 ⋅ 1 − 0 ) − 1 3 [ x 3 3 ] 1 e = e 3 3 − 1 9 ( e 3 − 1 ) = 3 e 3 9 − e 3 − 1 9 = 3 e 3 − e 3 + 1 9 = 2 e 3 + 1 9 Answer: A) 2 e 3 + 1 9
questions.solution: Solution I = ∫ 1 e x 2 ln ⁡ x d x Integration by parts: u = ln ⁡ x , d v = x 2 d x d u = 1 x d x , v = x 3 3 I = [ x 3 3 ln ⁡ x ] 1 e − ∫ 1 e x 3 3 ⋅ 1 x d x = [ x 3 3 ln ⁡ x ] 1 e − 1 3 ∫ 1 e x 2 d x = ( e 3 3 ⋅ 1 − 0 ) − 1 3 [ x 3 3 ] 1 e = e 3 3 − 1 9 ( e 3 − 1 ) = 3 e 3 9 − e 3 − 1 9 = 3 e 3 − e 3 + 1 9 = 2 e 3 + 1 9 Answer: A) 2 e 3 + 1 9
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
I=∫1ex2ln⁡xdx

Integration by parts: u=ln⁡x, dv=x2dx
du=1xdx, v=x33

I=[x33ln⁡x]1e−∫1ex33⋅1xdx

=[x33ln⁡x]1e−13∫1ex2dx

=(e33⋅1−0)−13[x33]1e

=e33−19(e3−1)

=3e39−e3−19=3e3−e3+19=2e3+19

Answer: A) 2e3+19

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, f ( x ) = e x ( sin ⁡ x − cos ⁡ x ) ∴ f ′ ( x ) = e x [ cos ⁡ x + sin ⁡ x ] + [ sin ⁡ x − cos ⁡ x ] e x ∴ f ′ ( x ) = 2 e x sin ⁡ x To verify Rolle's Theorem. f ′ ( c ) = 0 2 e c sin ⁡ c = 0 ⇒ sin ⁡ c = 0 ∴ c = π

questions.solutionText: Solution Let ( x 1 , y 1 ) be the required point ∴ 2 x 1 − y 1 = 5 Also, ( x 1 , y 1 ) is at the distance of 1 unit from line 3 x + 4 y = 5 ∴ 1 = | 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 − 5 9 + 16 | ∴ ± 5 = 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 − 5 ∴ 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 − 5 = 5 or 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 − 5 = − 5 ∴ 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 = 10 or 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 = 0 Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get x 1 = 30 11 and y 1 = 5 11 Solving equation (i) and (iii), we get x 1 = 20 11 and y 1 = − 15 11 ∴ ( 30 11 , 5 11 ) and ( 20 11 , − 15 11 ) are the required points.
questions.solution: Solution Let ( x 1 , y 1 ) be the required point ∴ 2 x 1 − y 1 = 5 Also, ( x 1 , y 1 ) is at the distance of 1 unit from line 3 x + 4 y = 5 ∴ 1 = | 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 − 5 9 + 16 | ∴ ± 5 = 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 − 5 ∴ 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 − 5 = 5 or 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 − 5 = − 5 ∴ 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 = 10 or 3 x 1 + 4 y 1 = 0 Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get x 1 = 30 11 and y 1 = 5 11 Solving equation (i) and (iii), we get x 1 = 20 11 and y 1 = − 15 11 ∴ ( 30 11 , 5 11 ) and ( 20 11 , − 15 11 ) are the required points.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let (x1,y1) be the required point

∴2x1−y1=5

Also, (x1,y1) is at the distance of 1 unit from line 3x+4y=5

∴1=|3x1+4y1−59+16|∴±5=3x1+4y1−5∴3x1+4y1−5=5 or 3x1+4y1−5=−5∴3x1+4y1=10 or 3x1+4y1=0

Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get x1=3011 and y1=511

Solving equation (i) and (iii), we get x1=2011 and y1=−1511

∴(3011,511) and (2011,−1511) are the required points.

questions.questionText: Let f x = x 2 , g x = log e ⁡ x . The number of values of x for which f o g x = g o f x is
questions.question: Let f x = x 2 , g x = log e ⁡ x . The number of values of x for which f o g x = g o f x is
questions.prompt: Let f x = x 2 , g x = log e ⁡ x . The number of values of x for which f o g x = g o f x is
questions.html: Let fx=x2, gx=loge⁡x. The number of values of x for which fogx=gofx is
questions.questionHtml: Let fx=x2, gx=loge⁡x. The number of values of x for which fogx=gofx is
questions.solutionText: Solution We have, f x = x 2 , g x = log e ⁡ x f o g x = f g x = f log e ⁡ x = log e ⁡ x 2 And g o f x = g f x = g x 2 = log e ⁡ x 2 Now, f o g x = g o f x ⇒ log e ⁡ x 2 = log e ⁡ x 2 ⇒ log e x 2 = 2 log e x ⇒ log e x 2 - 2 log e x = 0 ⇒ log e x log e x - 2 = 0 ⇒ log e x = 0 or log e x = 2 ⇒ x = e 2 or 1 So, there exists only two values of x .
questions.solution: Solution We have, f x = x 2 , g x = log e ⁡ x f o g x = f g x = f log e ⁡ x = log e ⁡ x 2 And g o f x = g f x = g x 2 = log e ⁡ x 2 Now, f o g x = g o f x ⇒ log e ⁡ x 2 = log e ⁡ x 2 ⇒ log e x 2 = 2 log e x ⇒ log e x 2 - 2 log e x = 0 ⇒ log e x log e x - 2 = 0 ⇒ log e x = 0 or log e x = 2 ⇒ x = e 2 or 1 So, there exists only two values of x .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

We have, fx=x2,gx=loge⁡x
fogx=fgx=floge⁡x=loge⁡x2
And gofx=gfx=gx2=loge⁡x2
Now, fogx=gofx⇒loge⁡x2=loge⁡x2

⇒logex2=2logex

⇒logex2-2logex=0

⇒logexlogex-2=0

⇒logex=0 or logex=2

⇒ x=e2 or 1
So, there exists only two values of x .

questions.text: The greatest and the least value of z 1 + z 2 if z 1 = 24 + 7 i and z 2 = 6 respectively are
questions.questionText: The greatest and the least value of z 1 + z 2 if z 1 = 24 + 7 i and z 2 = 6 respectively are
questions.question: The greatest and the least value of z 1 + z 2 if z 1 = 24 + 7 i and z 2 = 6 respectively are
questions.prompt: The greatest and the least value of z 1 + z 2 if z 1 = 24 + 7 i and z 2 = 6 respectively are
questions.html: The greatest and the least value of z1+z2 if z1 =24+7 i and z2=6 respectively are
questions.questionHtml: The greatest and the least value of z1+z2 if z1 =24+7 i and z2=6 respectively are
questions.solutionText: Solution z 1 = 24 + 7 i ⇒ | z 1 | = 24 2 + 7 2 = 576 + 49 = 625 = 25 | z 2 | = 6 By triangle inequality: | z 1 + z 2 | ≤ | z 1 | + | z 2 | = 25 + 6 = 31 | z 1 + z 2 | ≥ | | z 1 | − | z 2 | | = 25 − 6 = 19 Greatest value = 31 , Least value = 19 Answer: (1) 31, 19
questions.solution: Solution z 1 = 24 + 7 i ⇒ | z 1 | = 24 2 + 7 2 = 576 + 49 = 625 = 25 | z 2 | = 6 By triangle inequality: | z 1 + z 2 | ≤ | z 1 | + | z 2 | = 25 + 6 = 31 | z 1 + z 2 | ≥ | | z 1 | − | z 2 | | = 25 − 6 = 19 Greatest value = 31 , Least value = 19 Answer: (1) 31, 19
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
z1=24+7i ⇒ |z1|=242+72=576+49=625=25

|z2|=6

By triangle inequality:
|z1+z2|≤|z1|+|z2|=25+6=31
|z1+z2|≥||z1|−|z2||=25−6=19

Greatest value =31, Least value =19

Answer: (1) 31, 19

questions.text: The function f x = x 2 - a , x > 2 b - x , x ≤ 2 is continuous at the point x = 2 , then the value of a 2 + b 2 + 2 a b + 4 is
questions.questionText: The function f x = x 2 - a , x > 2 b - x , x ≤ 2 is continuous at the point x = 2 , then the value of a 2 + b 2 + 2 a b + 4 is
questions.question: The function f x = x 2 - a , x > 2 b - x , x ≤ 2 is continuous at the point x = 2 , then the value of a 2 + b 2 + 2 a b + 4 is
questions.prompt: The function f x = x 2 - a , x > 2 b - x , x ≤ 2 is continuous at the point x = 2 , then the value of a 2 + b 2 + 2 a b + 4 is
questions.html: The function fx=x2-a ,x>2b-x ,x≤2 is continuous at the point x=2, then the value of a2+b2+2ab+4 is
questions.questionHtml: The function fx=x2-a ,x>2b-x ,x≤2 is continuous at the point x=2, then the value of a2+b2+2ab+4 is
questions.solutionText: Solution We have, f x = x 2 - a , x > 2 b - x , x ≤ 2 As f ( x ) is continuous at x = 2 ⇒ lim x → 2 - f x = lim x → 2 + f x ⇒ b – 2 = 4 – a ⇒ a + b = 6 ⇒ a + b 2 = 36 ⇒ a 2 + b 2 + 2 a b + 4 = 36 + 4 = 40 .
questions.solution: Solution We have, f x = x 2 - a , x > 2 b - x , x ≤ 2 As f ( x ) is continuous at x = 2 ⇒ lim x → 2 - f x = lim x → 2 + f x ⇒ b – 2 = 4 – a ⇒ a + b = 6 ⇒ a + b 2 = 36 ⇒ a 2 + b 2 + 2 a b + 4 = 36 + 4 = 40 .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

We have, fx=x2-a ,x>2b-x ,x≤2
As f(x) is continuous at x=2
⇒limx→2-fx=limx→2+fx

⇒b –2=4 –a

⇒a+b=6

⇒a+b2 =36

⇒a2+b2+2ab+4=36+4=40.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.text: The equation ( k − 2 ) x 2 + ( 4 − k ) y 2 + ( k 2 − 4 ) x y − 4 x + 6 y + k = 0 represents a circle. Find the radius of this circle.
questions.questionText: The equation ( k − 2 ) x 2 + ( 4 − k ) y 2 + ( k 2 − 4 ) x y − 4 x + 6 y + k = 0 represents a circle. Find the radius of this circle.
questions.question: The equation ( k − 2 ) x 2 + ( 4 − k ) y 2 + ( k 2 − 4 ) x y − 4 x + 6 y + k = 0 represents a circle. Find the radius of this circle.
questions.prompt: The equation ( k − 2 ) x 2 + ( 4 − k ) y 2 + ( k 2 − 4 ) x y − 4 x + 6 y + k = 0 represents a circle. Find the radius of this circle.
questions.html: The equation (k−2)x2+(4−k)y2+(k2−4)xy−4x+6y+k=0 represents a circle. Find the radius of this circle.
questions.questionHtml: The equation (k−2)x2+(4−k)y2+(k2−4)xy−4x+6y+k=0 represents a circle. Find the radius of this circle.
questions.solutionText: Solution For a circle: coefficient of x 2 = coefficient of y 2 , and coefficient of x y = 0 Coeff of x y = 0 : k 2 − 4 = 0 ⇒ k = ± 2 Coeff of x 2 = coeff of y 2 : k − 2 = 4 − k ⇒ 2 k = 6 ⇒ k = 3 Conflict: k = 3 vs k = ± 2 . Try k = 2 : Coeff of x 2 = 0 , coeff of y 2 = 2 ⇒ not equal. Try k = − 2 : Coeff of x 2 = − 4 , coeff of y 2 = 6 ⇒ not equal. No value of k satisfies both conditions simultaneously. Answer: D) No such circle exists
questions.solution: Solution For a circle: coefficient of x 2 = coefficient of y 2 , and coefficient of x y = 0 Coeff of x y = 0 : k 2 − 4 = 0 ⇒ k = ± 2 Coeff of x 2 = coeff of y 2 : k − 2 = 4 − k ⇒ 2 k = 6 ⇒ k = 3 Conflict: k = 3 vs k = ± 2 . Try k = 2 : Coeff of x 2 = 0 , coeff of y 2 = 2 ⇒ not equal. Try k = − 2 : Coeff of x 2 = − 4 , coeff of y 2 = 6 ⇒ not equal. No value of k satisfies both conditions simultaneously. Answer: D) No such circle exists
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For a circle: coefficient of x2 = coefficient of y2, and coefficient of xy=0

Coeff of xy=0: k2−4=0⇒k=±2

Coeff of x2 = coeff of y2: k−2=4−k⇒2k=6⇒k=3

Conflict: k=3 vs k=±2. Try k=2:
Coeff of x2=0, coeff of y2=2 ⇒ not equal. Try k=−2:
Coeff of x2=−4, coeff of y2=6 ⇒ not equal.

No value of k satisfies both conditions simultaneously.

Answer: D) No such circle exists

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.text: The remainder when 7 n − 6 n − 50 ( n ∈ N ) is divided by 36 , is
questions.questionText: The remainder when 7 n − 6 n − 50 ( n ∈ N ) is divided by 36 , is
questions.question: The remainder when 7 n − 6 n − 50 ( n ∈ N ) is divided by 36 , is
questions.prompt: The remainder when 7 n − 6 n − 50 ( n ∈ N ) is divided by 36 , is
questions.html: The remainder when 7n−6n−50(n∈N) is divided by 36 , is
questions.questionHtml: The remainder when 7n−6n−50(n∈N) is divided by 36 , is
questions.solutionText: Solution 7 n = ( 1 + 6 ) n = 1 + 6 n + ( n 2 ) 6 2 + ( n 3 ) 6 3 + . . . 7 n − 6 n − 1 = ( n 2 ) 36 + ( n 3 ) 6 3 + . . . = 36 [ ( n 2 ) + ( n 3 ) 6 + . . . ] So 7 n − 6 n − 1 is divisible by 36. 7 n − 6 n − 50 = ( 7 n − 6 n − 1 ) − 49 ( 7 n − 6 n − 1 ) is divisible by 36, so remainder when 7 n − 6 n − 50 is divided by 36 is same as remainder when − 49 is divided by 36. − 49 = − 2 × 36 + 23 Remainder = 23 Answer: (2) 23
questions.solution: Solution 7 n = ( 1 + 6 ) n = 1 + 6 n + ( n 2 ) 6 2 + ( n 3 ) 6 3 + . . . 7 n − 6 n − 1 = ( n 2 ) 36 + ( n 3 ) 6 3 + . . . = 36 [ ( n 2 ) + ( n 3 ) 6 + . . . ] So 7 n − 6 n − 1 is divisible by 36. 7 n − 6 n − 50 = ( 7 n − 6 n − 1 ) − 49 ( 7 n − 6 n − 1 ) is divisible by 36, so remainder when 7 n − 6 n − 50 is divided by 36 is same as remainder when − 49 is divided by 36. − 49 = − 2 × 36 + 23 Remainder = 23 Answer: (2) 23
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
7n=(1+6)n=1+6n+(n2)62+(n3)63+...

7n−6n−1=(n2)36+(n3)63+...=36[(n2)+(n3)6+...]

So 7n−6n−1 is divisible by 36.

7n−6n−50=(7n−6n−1)−49

(7n−6n−1) is divisible by 36, so remainder when 7n−6n−50 is divided by 36 is same as remainder when −49 is divided by 36.

−49=−2×36+23

Remainder =23

Answer: (2) 23

questions.solutionText: Solution Since, the quadratic expression x 2 + 2 x + 2 = ( x + 1 ) 2 + 1 ∈ [ 1 , ∞ ) , ∀ x ∈ R ⇒ 1 ( x + 1 ) 2 + 1 ∈ ( 0 , 1 ] For f ( x ) = 1 x 2 + 2 x + 2 , ∀ x ∈ R is surjective, then set A = ( 0 , 1 ]

questions.solutionText: Solution We have to find locus of mid point of chord and we know perpendicular from centre bisects the chord. ⇒ ∠ O A C = 4 5 o O C O A = sin 4 5 o ⇒ O C = 2 2 = 2 Also, h 2 + k 2 = O C Hence, x 2 + y 2 = 2 is required equation of locus.

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
We have to find locus of mid point of chord and we know perpendicular from centre bisects the chord.




⇒∠OAC=45o

OCOA=sin45o ⇒ OC=22=2

Also,h2+k2=OC

Hence, x2+y2= 2 is required equation of locus.

( i ) and x 4 + y 4 = t 2 + 1 t 2 ⇒ x 4 + y 4 = t - 1 t 2 + 2 Since a + b 2 = a 2 + b 2 + 2 a b ⇒ x 4 + y 4 = x 2 + y 2 2 + 2 ⇒ x 4 + y 4 = x 4 + y 4 + 2 x 2 y 2 + 2 ∴ x 2 y 2 = - 1 On differentiation w.r.t. x : ⇒ x 2 . 2 y d y d x + y 2 . 2 x = 0 ⇒ x 3 y d y d x = - x 2 y 2 ⇒ x 3 y d y d x = - ( - 1 ) ⇒ x 3 y d y d x = 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given,
x2 + y2=t- 1t ... (i)

and
x4 + y4=t2 + 1t2⇒x4 + y4=t-1t2+2

Since

a+b2=a2+b2+2ab

⇒x4 + y4=x2 + y22 + 2⇒x4 + y4=x4 + y4 + 2x2y2 + 2

∴ x2y2=-1

On differentiation w.r.t. x:

questions.prompt: The solution of the differential equation d y d x = y c o s x - y 2 sin ⁡ x is equal to (where c is an arbitrary constant)
questions.html: The solution of the differential equation dydx = ycos x-y2sin⁡x is equal to (where c is an arbitrary constant)
questions.questionHtml: The solution of the differential equation dydx = ycos x-y2sin⁡x is equal to (where c is an arbitrary constant)
questions.solutionText: Solution d y d x = y c o s x - y 2 sin ⁡ x y cos ⁡ xdx - sin ⁡ x d y = y 2 d x y c o s x . d x - sin ⁡ x . d y y 2 = d x d sin ⁡ x y = d x On integrating, we get, sin ⁡ x y = x + c sin ⁡ x = x y + c y
questions.solution: Solution d y d x = y c o s x - y 2 sin ⁡ x y cos ⁡ xdx - sin ⁡ x d y = y 2 d x y c o s x . d x - sin ⁡ x . d y y 2 = d x d sin ⁡ x y = d x On integrating, we get, sin ⁡ x y = x + c sin ⁡ x = x y + c y

ycos x.dx-sin⁡x.dyy2=dx
dsin⁡xy=dx
